<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189147622 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00033: Block

- page_id: `189147622`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189147622/Block
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2018-03-21T13:41:58.870+01:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > User Guide > Glossary of SysML concepts > Element descriptions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Visibility representation

297287617

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Visibility representation

297287619

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Visibility representation

297287618

Blocks provide a general purpose capability to describe the architecture of a system and represent the system hierarchy in terms of systems and subsystems. Blocks describe not only the connectivity relationships within/between a system and its subsystems, but also quantitative values, as well as other information about that system (for example, documentation).

You can use SysML blocks throughout all phases of system specification and design, and apply them to many different kinds of systems. These include modeling either the logical or physical decomposition of a system, and the specification of software, hardware, or human elements.

A Block is a modular unit that describes the structure of a system or an element. It may include both structural and behavioral features (such as properties and operations) that represent the state of the system and behavior that the system may exhibit. Some of these properties may hold parts of a system that can also be described by blocks. A block may include a structure of connectors between its properties to indicate how its parts or other properties relate to one another.

Any reusable form of description that may be applied to a system or a set of system characteristics can be described by a block. These reusable descriptions, for example, may be applied to purely conceptual aspects of a system design, such as relationships that hold between parts or properties of a system. Parts (properties) in these systems can interact by many different means, such as software operations, discrete state transitions, flows of inputs and outputs, or continuous interactions. Connectors owned by SysML blocks can be used to define relationships between parts or other properties of the same containing block.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### NOTE: Visibility representation

Visibility representation

In SysML, properties and operations of the Block are public. Visibility representation literals, like +, #, ~, -, are not displayed in the Containment tree or in the element symbol on a diagram.

297287616

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Block Definition Diagram' space='SYSMLP2024xR2']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189148039 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00034: Block structure

- page_id: `189148039`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189148039/Block+structure
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2020-06-25T04:23:22.043+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > Exporting to External Simulation Models > Sample models > Creating a simple model > Blocks and Parts
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The three components are connected through ports that correspond electrical pins. The source and the resistor each have both a positive and a negative pin, whereas the ground has only one positive pin. Electrical energy (carried by electric charge) is transmitted between components through the pins. The image below contains the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Block Definition Diagram' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] that illustrates this.

A new abstract block *TwoPinComponent* is created to illustrate abstract components that have two ports (pins). These two ports are named *p*******and*n*, and they are both of the *ChargeFlowElement*type. ChargeFlowElement belongs to the simulation library (*SysPhSLibrary.mdzip*), and it can be selected by unchecking **Apply Filter**in the type list.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The *p* and*n* ports typed by *ChargeFlow Element*Interface Block.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The three components are connected through ports that correspond electrical pins. The source and the resistor each have both a positive and a negative pin, whereas the ground has only one positive pin. Electrical energy (carried by electric charge) is transmitted between components through the pins. The image below contains the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML Block Definition Diagram" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Block Definition Diagram (BDD)]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> that illustrates this. </p><p>A new abstract block <em>TwoPinComponent</em> is created to illustrate abstract components that have two ports (pins). These two ports are named <em>p</em><strong><em> </em> </strong>and<em> n</em>, and they are both of the <em>ChargeFlowElement </em>type. ChargeFlowElement belongs to the simulation library (<em>SysPhSLibrary.mdzip</em>), and it can be selected by unchecking <strong>Apply Filter  </strong>in the type list. </p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="470"><ri:attachment ri:filename="electrical_components.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Block structure" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span style="color: rgb(94,108,132);">The <em>p</em> and<em> n</em> ports typed by <em>ChargeFlow Element </em>Interface Block.</span></h6><p><br /></p><p />
````

## PAGE 00035: Blocks and Parts

- page_id: `189148035`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189148035/Blocks+and+Parts
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-01-29T11:36:18.681+01:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > Exporting to External Simulation Models > Sample models > Creating a simple model
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1553744160

1553744160

1553744160

The electrical circuit model has the main *block* and the*circuit*. To model the structure of the circuit, create a Circuit [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] and an associated [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Internal Block Diagram' space='SYSMLP2024xR2']. The circuit has three parts: *a source*, *a ground*, and *a resistor*. These parts are of a different type since they all have a different behavior. It is necessary to create a Block for each of these parts. The picture below shows how the Internal Block Diagram look like.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']The *Circuit* IBD consists of three parts: *source*, *ground* and *resistor*.

1553744159

**Related pages:**

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189147628 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00036: Bound Reference

- page_id: `189147628`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189147628/Bound+Reference
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2016-04-07T07:43:21.653+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > User Guide > Glossary of SysML concepts > Element descriptions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Properties with Bound Reference applied and upper multiplicity greater than one are ordered, with values ordered according to when they are reached in navigating the binding path (and how they are ordered within their blocks), and non-unique, to support paths that lead to or pass through the same object.

#### PANEL: Related diagrams

Related diagrams

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Internal Block Diagram' space='SYSMLP2024xR2']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189147629 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00037: Boundary System

- page_id: `189147629`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189147629/Boundary+System
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2016-04-08T08:45:47.459+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > User Guide > Glossary of SysML concepts > Element descriptions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A Boundary System is a special external system that serves as medium between another system and the system under development without having its own interests in the communication.

#### TIP: Example

Example

- Bus system.
- Communication system.

#### PANEL: Related diagrams

Related diagrams

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Use Case Diagram' space='SYSMLP2024xR2']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>A Boundary System is a special external system that serves as medium between another system and the system under development without having its own interests in the communication.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c6229ef5-b30b-4f77-a1cb-51877b301b63"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Bus system.</li><li>Communication system.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p> </p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p> </p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="de9adc89-d387-43d7-a667-124571a795a9"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related diagrams</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML Use Case Diagram" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189147497 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00038: BoundReference

- page_id: `189147497`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189147497/BoundReference
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2021-01-06T06:39:53.021+01:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > User Guide > Validation > SysML validation rules > Validation rules of Non-normative Extensions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Abbreviation**

BOUND_SYNC

**Description**

This rule checks if property stereotyped by the «boundReference» is the role of the connector end of at least one binding connector, or generalized by such a property through redefinition. The roles of the connector ends are specified in the **Bound End** property value. The value of boundEnd is a connector end of a binding connector.

**Severity**

warning****

**Constrained****element**

Property

**Solvers**

- Synchronize - synchronizes the Bound Reference property by setting a role of the connector end in the Bound End property value.

**Example**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The *chs.rb* role of the connector end is specified for the *rb* Bound Reference property.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><strong>Abbreviation</strong></p><p>BOUND_SYNC</p><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>This rule checks if property stereotyped by the «boundReference» is the role of the connector end of at least one binding connector, or generalized by such a property through redefinition. The roles of the connector ends are specified in the <strong>Bound End</strong> property value. The value of boundEnd is a connector end of a binding connector.</p><p><strong>Severity</strong></p><p>warning<strong><br /></strong></p><p><strong>Constrained</strong><strong> element</strong></p><p>Property</p><p><strong>Solvers</strong></p><ul><li><strong>Synchronize</strong> - synchronizes the Bound Reference property by setting a role of the connector end in the <strong>Bound End</strong> property value.</li></ul><p><strong>Example</strong></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="BOUND_SYNC.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="BoundReference" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The <em>chs.rb</em> role of the connector end is specified for the <em>rb</em> Bound Reference property.</h6><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189147988 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00039: Building Modelica libraries in SysML

- page_id: `189147988`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189147988/Building+Modelica+libraries+in+SysML
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-10-04T14:46:22.117+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > Exporting to External Simulation Models > Modelica export
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### Introduction

The Modelica library allows quickly to build SysML models for simulation by reusing library elements. It provides model components in many domains that are based on standardized interface definitions. The library components are ready to use and shareable between applications. [Find all modelica libraries >>](https://www.modelica.org/libraries)

##### Importing the Modelica Standard Library

The Modelica Standard Library brings the maincomponentsand standard component interfaces from many engineering domains. It provides theblack-boxes of Modelica blocks that contains only ports and parameters.The Modelica Standard Library components are imported to SysML project as follow:

- All Modelica types have «ModelicaBlock» stereotype applied with a full Modelica type name specified in the Name property.
- Modelica blocks are created as SysML Blocks.
- Modelica parameters are created as SysML Value Properties.
- Modelica connectors are created as SysML Interface Blocks.
- Primitive types are created as Value Types.
- If Modelica connector has property with flow keyword defined, it is created as SysML Flow Property for an Interface Block.
- For Block (defined as Model in Modelica) import and create only ports (no keyword, type is Modelica Connector/SysML InterfaceBlock) and parameters as ValueProperties.
- Units and quantities are mapped to ISO80000 units and quantities.

All modelica libraries depend from the Modelica Standard Library because of references between components.That's why the first thing you nee to do before using Modelica libraries in SysML projects, is to import the Modelica Standard Library.

impTo import Modelica Standard Library

1. [Download](https://github.com/modelica/ModelicaStandardLibrary/releases) the Modelica Standard Library and extract it.
2. Open a project in which you want to use the Modelica library components.
3. In the top-left corner of the modeling tool, click the**File**>**Import From**> **Modelica Library**.
4. Select the *ModelicaStandardLibrary > Modelica* folder from your file system.
5. Click **Import**.The Modelica Standard Library is imported. See the following figure.All packages with their structure is createdin the Containment treeand types are placed in corresponding packages. Note, that file names may do not match with the element names and the file structure may do not correspond to the location in the directory. The package structure is the same as Modelica package structure. In order to to avoid the import of Modelica Standard Library in each project, you can save it as a project and reuse in other projects. For this, do the following:[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Sharing project data' space='MD2024xR2']>]]>[https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR2/Sharing+project+data](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR2/Sharing+project+data)[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using other projects in a project' space='MD2024xR2']>]]> [IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Using Modelica libraries

Let's say we need to use *OpenHydraulics* modelica library components in a project. Both *Modelica Standard Library* and *OpenHydraulics* library**must be imported since some components from OpenHydraulics library have references to standard library.

To use the Modelica libraries in SysML project

1. Create new or open existing SysML project.
2. Import the Modelica Standard Library . >]]>
3. [Download](https://github.com/modelica-3rdparty/OpenHydraulics) the *OpenHydraulics*library and extract it.
4. I n the top-left corner of the modeling tool, click the File > Import From > Modelica Library .
5. Select the OpenHydraulics folder from your file system.
6. Click Import . 
The *OpenHydraulics* library is imported. The figure below demonstrates the reference between components from *OpenHydraulics* and *Modelica Standard Libraries*: the *RealOuput* type of a Proxy Port *y* which is in *OpenHydraulics* library references to *Modelica Standard Library*. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

You can create model by dragging Modelica library elements directly on diagram pane. As shown in the figure below, the simple schema is created by dragging *Capacitor* and *Resistor* Blocks on IBD diagram pane and displaying all their ports. To complete schema creation, you need to type names *c* and *r* for a Part Properties and connect ports.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

The figure below shows exported model to Modelica filewhich already contains references to the Modelica library components.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189146736 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00043: Changing the label display mode

- page_id: `189146736`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189146736/Changing+the+label+display+mode
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-10-04T14:46:02.334+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > User Guide > Modeling structure with Blocks > Connecting Blocks in SysML Internal Block Diagram > Implied Connectors
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1020905067

1020905069

1020912715

**On this page**

43

1020905068

The label of implied relation provides useful information about actual ports and Connectors. You can define how much of the information should be shown in the label by changing the **Label Display Mode**in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Symbol Properties dialog' space='MD2024xR2']**Symbol Properties** dialog.The procedure below describes how to change the label display mode and explains the meaning each of display mode.

To change the label display mode

1. Right-click the implied relation.
2. Select **Symbol Properties**.
3. In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Symbol Properties dialog' space='MD2024xR2']**Symbol Properties** dialog, find the **Label Display Mode** property and select one of the following value:
  - 
    - **Do Not Display** - to hide the label.
    - **Compact**(default) - to show a short summary in the label of how many actual connectors are underneath.
    - **Detailed** - to show all names or types of ports on both actual connector ends.
4. Click **OK**.

##### Compact Label Display Mode

It allows you to show a short summary in the label of how many actual connectors are underneath. It also displays names of the first level ports at both connector ends, if actual connectors exist only between two ports. Otherwise, the names will not be shown. The figure below demonstrates the situation, when all labels of implied relations provide a different information despite the same **Compact** display mode is selected for all of them.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Three labels of the implied relation with Compact display mode which provides different information.

1. The label of the implied relation between Part Property typed by *TV* Block and diagram frame indicates that only 1 actual connector exists between part and diagram frame because the number is not shown in brackets. The *power in* and socket 2 names in the label shows the names of the first level ports. 
2. The label of the implied relation between Part Properties typed by *TV* and *TV Tuner* Blocks indicates that 18 actual connectors exist between those parts. The *hdmi*name in the label shows the names of the first level ports. 
3. The label of the implied relation between Part Property typed by *TV Tuner* Block and diagram frame indicates that 9 actual connectors exist between part and diagram frame. The names of ports are not displayed in the label because actual connectors (which are underneath of implied relation) have different first level ports at both ends.

##### Detailed Label Display Mode

It allows you to show all names or types of ports on both actual connector ends. If actual Connectors arebetween two ports, the label is the same as**Compact** mode.The figure below demonstrates the situation, when all labels of implied relations provide a different information despite the same **Detailed** display mode is selected for all of them.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Three labels of the implied relation with Detailed display mode which provides different information.

In the figure above, the labels numbered 1. and 2.in the illustration, provide the same information as **Compact** display mode because actual connectors exist only between two ports. The label between the *TV Tuner* Block and diagram frame (3.) shows that 8 connectors exist between*internet* and *LAN socket* ports and 1 connector between *power in* and *socket 2* ports. It is useful to set the**Detailed**mode when you want to see all first level port names in the implied relation label (see figure below).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The implied relation with the Detailed label display mode.

1020905066

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4d928570-17ab-4746-ac1a-5f5151f14173"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1020905067</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5b1e0467-aa2c-4c84-81b4-86a9b3f6f53c"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1020905069</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6d226e83-60fa-4539-a42b-361d61345970"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1020912715</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="af235867-9cfa-4e16-acfc-9d4ea4c8430a"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">4</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="minLevel">3</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0582bdc4-7a75-4fa6-897e-6f64188795c5"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1020905068</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">The label of implied relation <span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">provides useful information <span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">about actual ports and Connectors</span></span>. You can define how much of the information should be shown in the label by changing <span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">the <strong>Label Display Mode </strong>in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR2" ri:content-title="Symbol Properties dialog" /><ac:link-body><strong>Symbol Properties</strong> dialog</ac:link-body></ac:link>.</span><span class="confluence-link"> </span>The procedure below describes how to change the label display mode and explains the meaning each of display mode.</span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">To change the label display mode</span></p><hr /><ol><li><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Right-click the implied relation.</span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Select <strong>Symbol Properties</strong>.<br /></span></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">In the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR2" ri:content-title="Symbol Properties dialog" /><ac:link-body><strong>Symbol Properties</strong> dialog</ac:link-body></ac:link>, f</span><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">ind the <strong>Label Display Mode</strong> property and select one of the following value:</span></p><ul><li style="list-style-type: none;background-image: none;"><ul><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><strong>Do Not Display</strong> - to hide the label.</span></p></li><li><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><strong>Compact </strong>(default) - to show a short summary in the label of how many actual connectors are underneath.<br /></span></p></li><li><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><strong>Detailed</strong> - to show all names or types of ports <span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">on both actual connector ends</span>.</span></p></li></ul></li></ul></li><li><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Click <strong>OK</strong>.</span></span></li></ol><h3><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Compact Label Display Mode</span></h3><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">It allows you to show a short summary in the label of how many actual connectors are underneath. It also displays names of the first level ports at both connector ends, if actual connectors exist only between two ports. Otherwise, the names will not be shown.</span> The figure below demonstrates the situation, when all labels of implied relations provide a different information despite the same <strong>Compact</strong> display mode is selected for all of them.</p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="compact_label_display_mode.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Three labels of the implied relation with Compact display mode which provides different information.</h6><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p>1. The label of the implied relation between Part Property typed by <em>TV</em> Block and diagram frame indicates that only 1 actual connector exists between part and diagram frame because the number is not shown in brackets. The <em>power in</em> and socket 2 names in the label shows the names of the first level ports.<br />2. The label of the implied relation between Part Properties typed by <em>TV</em> and <em>TV Tuner</em> Blocks indicates that 18 actual connectors exist between those parts. The <em>hdmi </em>name in the label shows the names of the first level ports.<br />3. The label of the implied relation between Part Property typed by <em>TV Tuner</em> Block and diagram frame indicates that 9 actual connectors exist between part and diagram frame. The names of ports are not displayed in the label because actual connectors (which are underneath of implied relation) have different first level ports at both ends.</p><h3><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Detailed <span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Label Display Mode</span> </span></h3><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">It allows you</span> to show all names or types of ports <span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">on both actual connector ends</span>. If actual Connectors are<span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"> between two ports</span>, the label is the same as<strong> Compact</strong> mode.<span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"> The figure below demonstrates the situation, when all labels of implied relations provide a different information despite the same <strong>Detailed</strong> display mode is selected for all of them.</span><br /></span></span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="detailed_label_display_mode.png" /></ac:image></span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Three labels of the implied relation with Detailed display mode which provides different information.</h6><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">In the figure above, the labels numbered <span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">1. and 2. </span>in the illustration, provide the same information as <strong>Compact</strong> display mode because <span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">actual connectors exist only between two ports</span>. The label between the <em>TV Tuner</em> Block and diagram frame (3.) shows that 8 connectors exist between<em> internet</em> and <em>LAN socket</em> ports and 1 connector between <em>power in</em> and <em>socket 2</em> ports. It is useful to set the<strong> Detailed </strong>mode when you want to see all first level port names in the implied relation label (see figure below).<br /></span></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Detailed label mode.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The implied relation with the Detailed label display mode.</h6><p><br <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189147499 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00044: ClassifierBehavior

- page_id: `189147499`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189147499/ClassifierBehavior
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2021-01-06T06:41:32.218+01:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > User Guide > Validation > SysML validation rules > Validation rules of Non-normative Extensions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Abbreviation**

CLASSIFIER_BEHAVIOR_SYNC

**Description**

This validation rule checks if property to which«classifierBehavior»stereotype is applied meet the following constraints:

- Is only applied to properties owned (not inherited) by Blocks that have classifier behaviors.
- Is composite.
- Is typed by the classifier behavior of their owning Block or a generalization of the classifier behavior.

**Severity**

warning****

**Constrained****element**

Property

**Solvers**

To fix this error, check if Classifier Behavior Property:

- Is Typed by the Activity.
- Aggregation property value set as composite .
- Have Name .
- Is owner by Blocks that have classifier behaviors.

**Example**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The **Aggregation******property value set to**composite** for the *Integrator1* Classifier Behavior.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189148045 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00045: Component Behavior

- page_id: `189148045`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189148045/Component+Behavior
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-01-29T11:38:56.138+01:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > Exporting to External Simulation Models > Sample models > Creating a simple model
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1553808215

1553808216

1553808216

Now that the variables and parameters are defined, the behavior of each block can be specified. To do so, equations are used to establish the relationships among different properties.

In SysML, these equations are represented as constraints within *ConstraintBlocks*. Their parameters correspond to the *PhSVariables* and *PhSConstant*of the blocks (“i”, “v”, “r”) as well as the*PhSVariables*present in the type of the ports (“pv”, “pi”, “nv”, “ni”). Refer to the image below to see how these constraints are represented in a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Block Definition Diagram' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'].

An abstract ConstraintBlock *TwoPinComponentConstraint* is created to gather parameters and equations common to the source and the resistor. These equations state that the voltage of the component is the difference between the voltage in the positive pin and the voltage in the negative pin. The current of the component is the current coming from the positive pin, and the current in the two pins must add up to zero. As for the ground, the constraint states that the voltage in the pin is zero.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Constraint Blocks with specified parameters and equations in BDD.

1553808214

**Related page:**

****

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

To do so, equations are used to establish the relationships among different properties.</p><p>In SysML, these equations are represented as constraints within <em>ConstraintBlocks</em>. Their parameters correspond to the <em>PhSVariables</em> and <em>PhSConstant  </em>of the blocks (“i”, “v”, “r”) as well as the<em> PhSVariables </em>present in the type of the ports (“pv”, “pi”, “nv”, “ni”). Refer to the image below to see how these constraints are represented in a <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML Block Definition Diagram" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Block Definition Diagram (BDD)]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><p>An abstract ConstraintBlock <em>TwoPinComponentConstraint</em> is created to gather parameters and equations common to the source and the resistor. These equations state that the voltage of the component is the difference between the voltage in the positive pin and the voltage in the negative pin. The current of the component is the current coming from the positive pin, and the current in the two pins must add up to zero. As for the ground, the constraint states that the voltage in the pin is zero.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="constraints.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Component Behavior" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Constraint Blocks with specified parameters and equations in BDD.</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="22bcc84a-0ee4-41c2-8c44-097a819f60f0"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1553808214</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page:</strong></p><p><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="d092f157-fedc-46f0-ab33-04ec2fc4b642" /><br /></strong></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189148042 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00046: Component variables and parameters

- page_id: `189148042`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189148042/Component+variables+and+parameters
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2020-06-25T04:24:57.676+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > Exporting to External Simulation Models > Sample models > Creating a simple model
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Each of the electrical component has variables corresponding to the domain being modeled. Electrical components have *voltage* and *current*. The voltage corresponds to the difference of potential between the positive and negative pin (across the component), whereas the current corresponds to the amount of charge going through the component.

Two properties *v* and*i*are created in*TwoPinComponent*. During the simulation, the voltage and the current will vary over time. As a result, the properties need to be stereotyped by *PhSVariable*. Their type must be a *ValueType* that has a unit with a symbol. Voltage and Current are already provided by the simulation library, so they can be selected (remove filters if necessary). The resistor has an additional property - the resistance. A property *r* is created in the resistor. Since the resistance will not change during the simulation, the property needs to be stereotyped by *PhSConstant.* It is necessary to create a new*ValueType* called *Resistance*that will serve as a type for this property. It must have a corresponding SysML *Unit* with a symbol set to *Ω*. Additionally, it must be a specialization of SysML*Real*.

The image below shows how [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Block Definition Diagram' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] should look like at this point.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The *i* and *v* Value properties typed by *Current* and *Voltage* value types.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Each of the electrical component has variables corresponding to the domain being modeled. Electrical components have <em>voltage</em> and <em>current</em>. The voltage corresponds to the difference of potential between the positive and negative pin (across the component), whereas the current corresponds to the amount of charge going through the component.</p><p>Two properties <em>v</em> and<em> i  </em>are created in<em> TwoPinComponent</em>. During the simulation, the voltage and the current will vary over time. As a result, the properties need to be stereotyped by <em>PhSVariable</em>. Their type must be a <em>ValueType</em> that has a unit with a symbol. Voltage and Current are already provided by the simulation library, so they can be selected (remove filters if necessary). The resistor has an additional property - the resistance. A property <em>r</em> is created in the resistor. Since the resistance will not change during the simulation, the property needs to be stereotyped by <em>PhSConstant.</em> It is necessary to create a new<em> ValueType</em> called <em>Resistance </em>that will serve as a type for this property. It must have a corresponding SysML <em>Unit</em> with a symbol set to <em>Ω</em>. Additionally, it must be a specialization of SysML<em> Real</em>.</p><p>The image below shows how <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML Block Definition Diagram" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Block Definition Diagram (BDD)]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> should look like at this point.</p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="component_variables_and_parameters.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Component variables and parameters" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span style="color: rgb(94,108,132);">The <em>i</em> and <em>v</em> Value properties typed by <em>Current</em> and <em>Voltage</em> value types.</span></h6><p />
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189147632 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00048: Conform

- page_id: `189147632`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189147632/Conform
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2016-04-08T11:19:07.763+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > User Guide > Glossary of SysML concepts > Element descriptions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A Conform relationship is a dependency between a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='View' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] and a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Viewpoint' space='SYSMLP2024xR2']. The View conforms to the rules and conventions specified in the Viewpoint.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### PANEL: Related diagram

Related diagram

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Views and Viewpoints Diagram' space='SYSMLP2024xR2']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>A Conform relationship is a dependency between a <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="View" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ac:link> and a <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Viewpoint" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ac:link>. The View conforms to the rules and conventions specified in the Viewpoint.</p><p><ac:image ac:alt="Conform" ac:title="Conform"><ri:attachment ri:filename="conform.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Conform" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p> </p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p> </p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="945c9ebe-05c7-4a1c-b7c9-5fdadbc65f5c"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related diagram</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Views and Viewpoints Diagram" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189147634 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00049: Conjugated Interface Block

- page_id: `189147634`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189147634/Conjugated+Interface+Block
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-10-04T14:46:15.356+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > User Guide > Glossary of SysML concepts > Element descriptions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

709525036

709525038

709525037

The Conjugated Interface Block (~Interface Block) is a specialization of [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Interface Block' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] that has the same features as its original Interface Block except that its [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Directed Feature' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Flow Property' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] are reversed (conjugated). As shown in the figure below, the*I/O*Interface Block is conjugated to ~I/O which has the same*Control and Status*Flow Properties but with opposite directions.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

709525034

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Block Definition Diagram' space='SYSMLP2024xR2']
- [Defining interfaces using ports](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/SYSMLP2024xR2/Defining+interfaces+using+ports)

ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ac:link> that has the same features as its original Interface Block except that its <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Directed Feature" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Directed Features]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> and <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Flow Property" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" the </span><em>I/O</em><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> Interface Block is conjugated to ~I/O which has the same </span><em>Control and Status</em><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> Flow <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189146707 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00050: Connecting Blocks in SysML Internal Block Diagram

- page_id: `189146707`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189146707/Connecting+Blocks+in+SysML+Internal+Block+Diagram
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-10-04T14:46:01.729+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > User Guide > Modeling structure with Blocks
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1022174050

1022174052

1022174051

After the composite [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space='']are created in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Block Definition Diagram' space=''], they can be instantiated on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Internal Block Diagram' space=''] as [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Part Property' space='']. These Part Properties are assembled through the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Connector' space='MD2024xR2'], linking them directly or via their ports.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']An internal structure of *Coffee Machine* Block where Part Properties are connected via Proxy Ports by using the Connector.

Read the following sections to learn how to connect Part Properties in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Internal Block Diagram' space='']:

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating a relationship' space='MD2024xR2']
- Connecting parts through interface
- Creating Item Flow
- Implied Connectors
- Autowiring function
- Enforce Ports Compatibility mode
- [Using Association Block as Connector type](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/SYSMLP2024xR2/Using+Association+Block+as+Connector+type)

49900651

**Sample model**

The model used in the figure of this page is the **Coffee Machine** sample model. To open this sample do one of the following:

- Download *[ATTACHMENT filename='CoffeeMachine.mdzip'].*
- Find in modeling tool <modeling tool installation directory>\samples\Simulation\CoffeeMachine.mdzip.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b1753659-2470-44b9-a0c5-12cf62f4588e"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1022174050</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="21821000-fdd6-4abd-b676-429550a85d67"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1022174052</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f66e0167-b77c-4fdb-98f4-33df3bb31769"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1022174051</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>After the composite <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Block" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Blocks ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>are created in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML Block Definition Diagram" /></ac:link>, they can be instantiated on the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML Internal Block Diagram" /></ac:link> as <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Part Property" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Part Properties]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. These Part Properties are assembled through the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR2" ri:content-title="Connector" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Connectors]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, linking them directly or via their ports.</p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ibd_example.png" /></ac:image>An internal structure of <em>Coffee Machine</em> Block where Part Properties are connected via Proxy Ports by using the Connector.</h6><p>Read the following sections to learn how to connect Part Properties in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML Internal Block Diagram" /></ac:link>:</p><ul><li><p class="with-breadcrumbs"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR2" ri:content-title="Creating a relationship" /></ac:link></p></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/SYSMLP2024xR2/Connecting+parts+through+interface">Connecting parts through interface</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/SYSMLP2024xR2/Creating+Item+Flow">Creating Item Flow</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/SYSMLP2024xR2/Implied+Connectors">Implied Connectors</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/SYSMLP2024xR2/Autowiring+function">Autowiring function</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/SYSMLP2024xR2/Enforce+Ports+Compatibility+mode">Enforce Ports Compatibility mode</a></li><li><p class="with-breadcrumbs"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/SYSMLP2024xR2/Using+Association+Block+as+Connector+type">Using Association Block as Connector type</a></p></li></ul><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="692c4c1c-f941-454b-9637-77f5cf50c1b8"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">49900651</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Sample model</strong></p><p>The model used in the figure of this page is the <strong>Coffee Machine</strong> sample model. To open this sample do one of the following:</p><ul><li>Download<span class="confluence-link"><em><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"> <ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="CoffeeMachine.mdzip"><ri:page ri:content-title="_sample models" /></ri:attachment></ac:link>.<br /></span></em></span></li><li>Find in modeling tool <em><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">&lt;modeling tool installation directory&gt;\samples\Simulation\CoffeeMachine.mdzip.</span></em></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189146709 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00051: Connecting parts through interface

- page_id: `189146709`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189146709/Connecting+parts+through+interface
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-10-04T14:46:01.808+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > User Guide > Modeling structure with Blocks > Connecting Blocks in SysML Internal Block Diagram
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can drag the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Interface Block' space=''] directly on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Connector' space='MD2024xR2']relationship to:

- Create [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Proxy Port' space=''] at both ends of the Connector.
- Set types (interfaces) of Proxy Ports.
- Set directions of Proxy Ports.
- Keep layout of the Connector .

That helps to ensure an efficient interface modeling in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Internal Block Diagram' space=''].

To connect [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Part Property' space=''] through interface

1. Create a Connector between two [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Part Property' space=''] .
2. Drag an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Interface Block' space=''] on the Connector.
3. From the opened menu, select Connect Through Interface command. The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Proxy Port' space=''] with directions and interfaces are created. [ATTACHMENT filename='dnd_on_connector.png']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>You can drag the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Interface Block" /></ac:link> directly on the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR2" ri:content-title="Connector" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Connector ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>relationship to:</p><ul><li>Create <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Proxy Port" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Proxy Ports]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> at both ends of the Connector.</li><li>Set types (interfaces) of Proxy Ports.</li><li>Set directions of Proxy Ports.</li><li>Keep <span style="color: rgb(255,0,0);"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">layout of the Connector</span></span>.</li></ul><p>That helps to ensure an efficient interface modeling in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML Internal Block Diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Internal Block Diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><p> </p><p>To connect <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Part Property" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[parts]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> through interface</p><hr /><ol><li>Create a Connector between two <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Part Property" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Part Properties]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li><li>Drag an <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Interface Block" /></ac:link> on the Connector.</li><li>From the opened menu, select <strong>Connect Through Interface</strong> command.<br />The <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Proxy Port" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Proxy Ports]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> with directions and interfaces are created.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="dnd_on_connector.png" /></ac:image></li></ol><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189147437 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00052: Connector Property

- page_id: `189147437`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189147437/Connector+Property
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2021-01-12T13:11:48.468+01:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > User Guide > Validation > SysML validation rules > Validation rules of Blocks
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Abbreviation**

ConnectorProperty

**Description**

This validation rule checks if the Connector Property is connected with the matching Connector.

**Severity**

warning****

**Constrained Element**

Connector Property****

**Solvers**

- Synchronize - reconnects the Connector Property to matching Connector in the model.

**Example**

**[IMAGE alt='' src='']**

###### The *Shower Water Delivery* Connector Property is synchronized with the*shawerWaterDelivery* Connector.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><strong>Abbreviation</strong></p><p>ConnectorProperty</p><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>This validation rule checks if the Connector Property is connected with the matching Connector. </p><p><strong>Severity</strong></p><p>warning<strong><br /></strong></p><p><strong>Constrained Element</strong></p><p>Connector Property<strong><br /></strong></p><p><strong>Solvers</strong></p><ul><li><strong>Synchronize </strong>- <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="599961ec-d644-4e98-bf3b-51e251f5d5cf">reconnects the Connector Property to matching Connector in the model.</ac:inline-comment-marker><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="599961ec-d644-4e98-bf3b-51e251f5d5cf"><br /></ac:inline-comment-marker></li></ul><p><strong>Example</strong></p><p><strong><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ConnectorProperty.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Connector Property" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></strong></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The <em>Shower Water Delivery</em> Connector Property is synchronized with the<em> shawerWaterDelivery</em> Connector.</h6>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189148037 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00053: Connectors

- page_id: `189148037`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189148037/Connectors
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2020-06-25T06:58:45.567+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > Exporting to External Simulation Models > Sample models > Creating a simple model > Blocks and Parts
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

In the Circuit [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Internal Block Diagram' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'], it is now possible to display the ports on the parts and connect them using [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Binding Connector' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] (see the figure below).

Ports can be displayed in IBD by selecting all existing parts, then right-clicking and from the shortcut menuselecting the **Related Elements** >**Display Ports**.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Three parts are connected trough the *p and n* ports.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>In the Circuit <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML Internal Block Diagram" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Internal Block Diagram (IBD)]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, it is now possible to display the ports on the parts and connect them using <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Binding Connector" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Connectors]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> (see the figure below).</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="06ffada6-f2b1-4511-a1d2-2a132a6beda5"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Ports can be displayed in IBD by selecting all existing parts, then right-clicking and <span>from the shortcut menu </span>selecting the <strong>Related Elements</strong> &gt;<strong> Display Ports</strong>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="250"><ri:attachment ri:filename="internal_structure_of_circuit.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Connectors" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Three parts are connected trough the <em>p and n</em> ports.</h6><p />
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189147636 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00054: Constraint Block

- page_id: `189147636`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189147636/Constraint+Block
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2020-09-25T11:41:55.943+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > User Guide > Glossary of SysML concepts > Element descriptions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Visibility representation

299826927

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Visibility representation

299826930

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Visibility representation

299826929

Constraint blocks provide a mechanism for integrating engineering analysis, such as performance and reliability models, with other SysML models. You can use Constraint blocks to specify a network of constraints that represent mathematical expressions, such as {tp=whlpwr-(Cd*v)-(Cf*tw*v)}, that constrain the physical properties of a system.

Such constraints can also be used to identify critical performance parameters and their relationships to other parameters that can be tracked throughout the system life cycle. A constraint block includes constraints (such as {tp=whlpwr-(Cd*v)-(Cf*tw*v)}) and their parameters (such as Cd, Cf, and i). Constraint blocks define generic forms of constraints that can be used in multiple contexts.

Reusable constraint definitions can be specified on Block Definition Diagrams and packaged into general-purpose or domain-specific model libraries.These constraints can be arbitrarily complex mathematical or logical expressions. The constraints can be nested to enable a constraint to be defined in terms of more basic constraints such as primitive mathematical operators.

In general, you should define constraints in constraint blocks in a Block Definition Diagram first, and then use a Parametric Diagram to bind constraint parameters to properties.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### NOTE: Visibility representation

Visibility representation

In SysML, properties and operations of the Block are public. Visibility representation literals, like +, #, ~, -, are not displayed in the Containment tree or in the element symbol on a diagram.

299826926

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Block Definition Diagram' space='SYSMLP2024xR2']

****

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="60d946d5-b989-464b-b21e-00a4f3913c5e"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">299826927</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f721249a-da24-406a-86ff-dfe4d36d2b67"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">299826930</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5f94b0ff-0fd4-465d-9fdb-95e3dbef0b87"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">299826929</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Constraint blocks provide a mechanism for integrating engineering analysis, such as performance and reliability models, with other SysML models. You can use Constraint blocks to specify a network of constraints that represent mathematical expressions, such as {tp=whlpwr-(Cd*v)-(Cf*tw*v)}, that constrain the physical properties of a system.</p><p>Such constraints can also be used to identify critical performance parameters and their relationships to other parameters that can be tracked throughout the system life cycle. A constraint block includes constraints (such as {tp=whlpwr-(Cd*v)-(Cf*tw*v)}) and their parameters (such as Cd, Cf, and i). Constraint blocks define generic forms of constraints that can be used in multiple contexts.</p><p>Reusable constraint definitions can be specified on Block Definition Diagrams and packaged into general-purpose or domain-specific model libraries.These constraints can be arbitrarily complex mathematical or logical expressions. The constraints can be nested to enable a constraint to be defined in terms of more basic constraints such as primitive mathematical operators.</p><p>In general, you should define constraints in constraint blocks in a Block Definition Diagram first, and then use a Parametric Diagram to bind constraint parameters to properties.</p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="constraint.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Constraint Block" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="45e244e1-7a14-47d4-88f2-ec499c585171"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Visibility representation</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">In SysML, properties and operations of the Block are public. Visibility representation literals, like +, #, ~, -, are not displayed in the Containment tree or in the element symbol on a diagram.</span><br /></span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8966fe55-e5c5-4829-844c-884a8ada30f6"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">299826926</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML Block Definition Diagram" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[SysML Block Definition Diagram ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ul><p><strong><br /></strong></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189147638 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00055: Constraint Parameter

- page_id: `189147638`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189147638/Constraint+Parameter
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-04-11T12:12:53.473+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > User Guide > Glossary of SysML concepts > Element descriptions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A**Constraint Parameter** is the formal term for a variable that appears in a constraint expression. It is displayed as a small square attached to the boundary on the inside of a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Constraint Property' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'].

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### PANEL: Related diagrams

Related diagrams

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Internal Block Diagram' space='SYSMLP2024xR2']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Parametric Diagram' space='SYSMLP2024xR2']

#### PANEL: Related pages

Related pages

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189148047 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00056: Constraint properties and bindings

- page_id: `189148047`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189148047/Constraint+properties+and+bindings
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2020-06-25T06:23:03.810+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > Exporting to External Simulation Models > Sample models > Creating a simple model > Component Behavior
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The previously created constraints need to be bound to the variable of each block. To do so, constraint properties are created in each block, and the block variables and parameters are bound to the constraint parameters accordingly. Images bellow show the bindings for the ground, the source, and the resistor respectively.

To create bindings, it is necessary to display the ports as well as their parts using the shortcut menu (**Related Elements** > **Display Ports**and **Related Elements** >**Display Parts**).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The SysML Parametric Diagram of *Ground* Block.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The SysML Parametric Diagram of *Source*Block.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The SysML Parametric Diagram of *Resistor*Block.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The previously created constraints need to be bound to the variable of each block. To do so, constraint properties are created in each block, and the block variables and parameters are bound to the constraint parameters accordingly. Images bellow show the bindings for the ground, the source, and the resistor respectively. </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5e8435b0-85a2-48c8-a679-58e30d196f8c"><ac:rich-text-body><p>To create bindings, it is necessary to display the ports as well as their parts using the shortcut menu (<strong>Related Elements</strong> &gt; <strong>Display Ports </strong>and <strong>Related Elements</strong> &gt;<strong> Display Parts</strong>).</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="680"><ri:attachment ri:filename="parametric_diagram_of_the_ground.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Constraint properties and bindings" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The SysML Parametric Diagram of <em>Ground</em> Block.</h6><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="680"><ri:attachment ri:filename="parametric_diagram_of_the_source.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Constraint properties and bindings" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span style="color: rgb(94,108,132);">The SysML Parametric Diagram of <em>Source </em>Block.</span></h6><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="680"><ri:attachment ri:filename="parametric_diagram_of_the_resistor.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Constraint properties and bindings" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span style="color: rgb(94,108,132);">The SysML Parametric Diagram of <em>Resistor </em></span><span style="color: rgb(94,108,132);">Block.</span></h6><p />

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189147640 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00057: Constraint Property

- page_id: `189147640`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189147640/Constraint+Property
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-10-04T14:46:15.513+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > User Guide > Glossary of SysML concepts > Element descriptions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1344959267

1344959270

1344959269

A Constraint Property is a property that specifies the constraints of other properties in its containing Block. Every Constraint Property has composite AggregationKind and is typed by a Constraint Block. Constraint Properties are displayed in the **constraints** compartment on a Block shape.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']The Constraint Property shape containing information of its name, type and expression.

To empower the constraint expression to perform calculations, you have to specify what system parameters should be consumed as variables of that constraint.You can define the Constraint expression as:

- Mathematical equation. E.g. {area =3.14159 * (radius^2)}. When evaluating such equations, the simulation mechanism solves values to correspond the equation. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Mathematical equation' space='CST2024xR2']]]>
- Logical expression that contains a comparison operator: ==, <, >, <=, >=, and <>. E.g. {*val1 >= "500"}.*When evaluating such expressions with Boolean data type, the the simulation mechanism check the values to meet the expression. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Logical expression' space='CST2024xR2']]]>

1344959266

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Parametric Diagram' space='SYSMLP2024xR2']
- [Modeling parametric constraints](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/SYSMLP2024xR2/Modeling+parametric+constraints)

Every Constraint Property has composite AggregationKind and is typed by a Constraint Block. Constraint Properties are displayed in the <strong>constraints</strong> compartment on a Block shape. </p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="constraint_property.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Constraint Property" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><span style="color: rgb(94,108,132);">The Constraint Property shape containing information of its name, type and expression.</span></h6><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> </span><span style="color: rgb(9,30,66);">To empower the constraint expression to perform calculations, you have to specify what system parameters should be consumed as variables of that constraint. </span>You can define the Constraint expression as:</p><ul><li><p class="with-breadcrumbs">Mathematical equation. E.g. {area =<span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> </span>3.14159 * (radius<span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> </span>^<span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> </span>2)}. When evaluating such equations, the simulation mechanism solves values to correspond the equation. <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024xR2" ri:content-title="Mathematical equation" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn more about evaluating mathematical equations >]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></p></li><li><p class="with-breadcrumbs">Logical expression that <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">contains a comparison operator: ==, &lt;, &gt;, &lt;=, &gt;=, and &lt;&gt;.</span> E.g. {<em>val1 &gt;= &quot;500&quot;}. </em>When evaluating such expressions with Boolean data type, the the simulation mechanism check the values to meet the expression. <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024xR2" ri:content-title="Logical expression" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn more about evaluating logical expressions >]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></p></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="eda376be-03d4-4747-9add-b8570a98a008"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1344959266</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML Parametric Diagram" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ac:link></li><li><p class="with-breadcrumbs"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/SYSMLP2024xR2/Modeling+parametric+constraints" class="current">Modeling parametric constraints</a></p></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189146907 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00059: Contextual relationships

- page_id: `189146907`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189146907/Contextual+relationships
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2021-08-02T17:34:58.644+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > User Guide
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Contextual relationships provide the ability to define the specific context that relationships are used in. Before you can create contextual relationships in your project, you must enable their creation in the **Project Options** dialog.

There are five types of relationships you can contextualize: *Allocate*, *Refine*, *Satisfy*, *Trace*, *and Verify*.

To enable the creation of contextual relationships

1. Click Options > Project .
2. Under General , click SysML .
3. Select Creation of Contextual Relationships .
4. Choose Fully Enabled , Partially Enabled , or Disabled from the drop-down menu on the right.

| Fully EnabledFully Enabled | Create Contextual Relationships using the Create Relation dialog.Add a new Contextual Relations category to the Dependency Criteria dialog.Contextual Relations can only be created in dependency matrices with the specified Structure-tree view.Contextualize relationships while creating them on the diagram pane.Contextualize relationships created by drag-and-dropping elements directly from the Structure tab.Contextualize/decontextualize relationships using the smart manipulator toolbar. |
| --- | --- |
| Partially Enabled | Includes the same features as the Fully Enabled option (), excluding the ability to contextualize relationships using the diagram palette. |
| Disabled | Completely disable the creation of Contextual Relationships. |

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Contextual Relationships Options

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

/></li></ol><table class="relative-table" style="width: 99.616%;"><colgroup><col style="width: 11.7906%;" /><col style="width: 88.2094%;" /></colgroup><tbody><tr><td><div Enabled</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>Fully Enabled</strong></p></div></td><td><div class="content-wrapper"><ul><li>Create Contextual Relationships using the Create Relation dialog.</li><li>Add a new Contextual Relations category to the Dependency Criteria dialog.</li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="395a0a7a-50d4-41e7-9bbe-6b3ef7f1f9c7"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Contextual Relations can only be created in dependency matrices with the specified <strong>Structure</strong>-tree relationships" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Contextual Relationships Options</h6><p />

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This section explains the creation of a basic electrical circuit model using the SysML extension. The model can be exported into Modelica® and Simulink®. The same process can be used to create more complex models.

Read the following topics:

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189146619 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00064: Creating a SysML Internal Block diagram for a Block

- page_id: `189146619`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189146619/Creating+a+SysML+Internal+Block+diagram+for+a+Block
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2021-07-27T15:24:36.079+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > User Guide > Modeling structure with Blocks > Decomposing Blocks
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To create a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Internal Block Diagram' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] for a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space='SYSMLP2024xR2']

1. Select the Block shape on the diagram pane. The smart manipulator menu appears.
2. Click the SysML Internal Block diagram button [ATTACHMENT filename='ibd_icon.png'] . The SysML Internal Block diagram is created and owned by the selected Block. The SysML Internal Block diagram and the owner Block have the same name. The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Defining hyperlinks' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] to the created diagram is added to the Block.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>To create a <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML Internal Block Diagram" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[SysML Internal Block diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> for a <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Block" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ac:link></p><hr /><ol><li>Select the Block shape on the diagram pane. The smart manipulator menu appears.</li><li>Click the SysML Internal Block diagram button <ac:image ac:title="SysML Internal Block Diagram" ac:thumbnail="true" ac:alt="SysML Internal Block Diagram" ac:width="20"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ibd_icon.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating a SysML Internal Block diagram for a Block" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>.<br />The SysML Internal Block diagram is created and owned by the selected Block. The SysML Internal Block diagram and the owner Block have the same name. The <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Defining hyperlinks" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[hyperlink ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>to the created diagram is added to the Block.</li></ol><p><br /></p>
````
