<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189146775 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00093: Customizing Units

- page_id: `189146775`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189146775/Customizing+Units
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-10-04T14:46:03.014+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > User Guide > Modeling structure with Blocks > Using Units
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

If you did not find a suitable [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using Units' space=''] in the standard libraries (ISO-80000), you can:

3

##### Creating a custom Value Type

If you want to use a different [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Value Type' space=''] than our library provides, you can create a custom [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Value Type' space=''] and specify a desired [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using Units' space=''] for it from the ISO-80000 package.

To create a custom Value Type

1. Select a package in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Containment tab' space='MD2024xR2'] where you want to create a new Value Type.
2. Right-click it and select Create Element .
3. In the opened dialog, search for a Value Type and select it.
4. Type its name. Following syntax is recommended : quantity name[unit name] (e.g. volume[liter]).
5. Press Enter.
6. Double-click the newly created Value Type to open its [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024xR2'] .
7. In the Specification window, specify the Unit property value: 
 a. Click [IMAGE alt='' src='']. 
 b. In the **Select Instance Specification** dialog, clear the **Apply Filter** box. 
 c. Make sure the ISO-80000 library is loaded in your project. WarningIf the ISO-80000 library is not uploaded in your project, you can load it directly in the **Select Instance Specification** dialog by clicking the **Load** button. d. Select the required Unit from the ISO-80000 package (e.g., liter).
8. Click Close. The new Value Type is defined in your project and can be used as a type for a Value Property.

##### Creating a custom Unit

If you are unable to find a desired unit you can create a custom Unit. It is recommended that a[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using Units' space='']be related to a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Quantity Kind' space='']. Often, equations can be expressed in terms of quantities that include Quantity Kinds without specifying units.

To create a custom Unit

1. From the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Internal Block Diagram' space=''] palette, expand the **Value Type** button and select the **Unit** button. If you do not see the Unit button, make sure the **Expert Mode** is turned on.
2. Click the mouse pointer on the diagram pane. The Select Classifier dialog opens automatically.
3. In the Select Classifier dialog, find the Unit Block, double-click it to select it, and click OK . The new Unit is created.
4. Type its name (e.g., mile).
5. Double-click the Unit shape to open its [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024xR2'] .
6. In the Specification window, specify: The new Unit is created and can now be specified for the Value Type and then used in your project as a type of Value Property.
  - The **Quantity Kind** property value: 
 a. Click [IMAGE alt='' src='']. 
 b. In the **Select Quantity Kind** dialog, clear the **Apply Filter** box. 
 c. Make sure the ISO-80000 library is loaded in your project. WarningIf the ISO-80000 library is not uploaded in your project, you can load it directly in the **Select Quantity Kind** dialog by clicking the **Load** button. d. Select the required quantity from the ISO-80000 package (e.g., distance).
  - The symbol of Unit: a. In the Property group list , select Slots. b. Select the symbol slot and click the Create Value button. c. Type the unit symbol in the Value area (e.g., mi). d. Click Close .

Following are the QUDV library Classifiers that support [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Unit Conversion' space='CST2024xR2'].

- SimpleUnit – Base unit that other units can convert to.
- PrefixedUnit – Unit that converts to other units via prefix (for example kilo, mega, etc.).
- LinearConversionUnit – Unit that converts using predefined factor. Formula: ReferenceUnit=value*factor.
- AffineConversionUnit – Unit that converts using a predefined factor and offset. Formula: ReferenceUnit=value*factor+offset.
- DerivedUnit – Combination of several units.

After the classifier is defined, the slot window is available. The Slot window is used to define properties. Following are the properties that support Unit Conversion:

- referenceUnit – A unit to convert to. Can be a SimpleUnit or another ConversionUnit.
- prefix – Selecting a prefix suitable for the unit (only for PrefixedUnit).
- factor – Conversion factor (for LinearConversion and AffineConversion Units).
- offset – Static part for AffineConversionUnits.

##### Derived Unit

Derived units are a combination of several pre-defined units. Creating a Derived Unit follows a different logic than other types of units. Derived Units do not have a referenceUnit slot.

The factor slot is filled with UnitFactors. A UnitFactor is an instance that has a Unit and an Exponent. For example, a DerivedUnit of m/s^2 (acceleration) would have a factor of m^1 (meter with exponent 1) and s^-2 (second with exponent -2).

If the required UnitFactor is not found, then you need to create a new UnitFactor and use it to create new derived units.

To create a UnitFactor

1. Create a new Instance Specification.
2. Set its classifier to UnitFactor.
3. Fill in the values of Unit and Exponent slots.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### An example of a derived unit(acceleration).

To learn more about derived units, refer to [OMG SysML Specification](https://www.omg.org/spec/SysML).

**Related pages**

- SysML Block Definition Diagram
- Instance Table
- Package import
- Using QUDV model library
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using Units' space='']
- Quantity Kind
- Value Type
- Value Property
- Block

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><span>If you did not find a suitable <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Using Units" /></ac:link> in the standard libraries (ISO-80000), you can:</span></span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><span><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cc2f3d26-5df3-4c96-a7ce-4bf443d7ec20"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">3</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro><br /></span></span></p><h3>Creating a custom Value Type</h3><p>If you want to use a different <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Value Type" /></ac:link> than our library provides, you can create a custom <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Value Type" /></ac:link> and specify a desired <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Using Units" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[ unit]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> for it from the ISO-80000 package.</p><p>To create a custom Value Type</p><hr /><ol><li>Select a package in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR2" ri:content-title="Containment tab" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Containment tree]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> where you want to create a new Value Type.</li><li>Right-click it and select <strong>Create Element</strong>.</li><li>In the opened dialog, search for a Value Type and select it.</li><li><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">Type its name. Following syntax is recommended</span>: quantity name[unit name] (e.g. volume[liter]).</li><li>Press Enter.</li><li>Double-click the newly created <span class="confluence-link">Value Type</span> to open its <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR2" ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link>.</li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">In the Specification window, specify the Unit property value:<br /> a. Click <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="edit_value_button_in_specification_window.png" /></ac:image>.<br /> b. In the <strong>Select Instance Specification</strong> dialog, clear the <strong>Apply Filter</strong> box.<br /> c. Make sure the ISO-80000 library is loaded in your project.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d48ecb50-a766-4d5d-96bd-b6371cd1461f"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Warning</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>If the ISO-80000 library is not uploaded in your project, you can load it directly in the <strong>Select Instance Specification</strong> dialog by clicking the <strong>Load</strong> button.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"> d. Select the required Unit from the ISO-80000 package (e.g., liter).</p></li><li>Click <strong>Close.</strong><br />The new Value Type is defined in your project and can be used as a type for a Value Property.</li></ol><h3>Creating a custom Unit</h3><p><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">If you are unable to find a desired unit</span> you can create a custom Unit. It is recommended that a<span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><span> <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Using Units" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[unit]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);"> </span><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="9486fada-b272-4d66-b4d5-8902aeb867f0">be</ac:inline-comment-marker> related to a <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Quantity Kind" /></ac:link>. Often, equations can be expressed in terms of quantities that include Quantity Kinds without specifying units.</span></span></p><p>To create a custom Unit</p><hr /><ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">From the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML Internal Block Diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Block Definition Diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> palette, expand the <strong>Value Type</strong> button and select the <strong>Unit</strong> button.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ee474415-38c3-482f-96e8-2353a80b1b22"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If you do not see the Unit button, make sure the <strong>Expert Mode</strong> is turned on.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li>Click the mouse pointer on the diagram pane. The <strong>Select Classifier</strong> dialog opens automatically.</li><li>In the <strong>Select Classifier</strong> dialog, find the <em>Unit</em> Block, double-click it to select it, and click <strong>OK</strong>. The new Unit is created.</li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Type its name (e.g., mile).</p></li><li>Double-click the Unit shape to open its <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR2" ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link>.</li><li>In the Specification window, specify: <br /><ul><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">The <strong>Quantity Kind</strong> property value:<br /> a. Click <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="edit_value_button_in_specification_window.png" /></ac:image>.<br /> b. In the <strong>Select Quantity Kind</strong> dialog, clear the <strong>Apply Filter</strong> box.<br /> c. Make sure the ISO-80000 library is loaded in your project.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="76e9312f-7786-4012-970f-a09c16d90e4a"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Warning</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>If the ISO-80000 library is not uploaded in your project, you can load it directly in the <strong>Select Quantity Kind</strong> dialog by clicking the <strong>Load</strong> button.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"> d. Select the required quantity from the ISO-80000 package (e.g., distance).</p></li><li>The symbol of Unit:<br /> a. In the <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR2/Specification+window">Property group list</a>, select Slots.<br /> b. Select the symbol slot and click the <strong>Create Value</strong> button.<br /> c. Type the unit symbol in the Value area (e.g., mi).<br /> d. Click <strong>Close</strong>.</li></ul>The new Unit is created and can now be specified for the Value Type and then used in your project as a type of Value Property.</li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5dac77f7-e789-4872-8608-a7cadd8951bf"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Following are the QUDV library Classifiers that support <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024xR2" ri:content-title="Unit Conversion" /></ac:link>.</p><ul><li>SimpleUnit – Base unit that other units can convert to.</li><li>PrefixedUnit – Unit that converts to other units via prefix (for example kilo, mega, etc.).</li><li>LinearConversionUnit – Unit that converts using predefined factor. Formula: ReferenceUnit=value*factor.</li><li>AffineConversionUnit – Unit that converts using a predefined factor and offset. Formula: ReferenceUnit=value*factor+offset.</li><li>DerivedUnit – Combination of several units.</li></ul><p>After the classifier is defined, the slot window is available. The Slot window is used to define properties. <span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Following are the properties that support Unit Conversion:</span></p><ul><li>referenceUnit – A unit to convert to. Can be a SimpleUnit or another ConversionUnit.</li><li>prefix – Selecting a prefix suitable for the unit (only for PrefixedUnit).</li><li>factor – Conversion factor (for LinearConversion and AffineConversion Units).</li><li>offset – Static part for AffineConversionUnits.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Derived Unit</h3><p>Derived units are a combination of several pre-defined units. Creating a Derived Unit follows a different logic than other types of units. Derived Units <span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">do not have a referenceUnit slot.</span></p><p>The factor slot is filled with UnitFactors. A UnitFactor is an instance that has a Unit and an Exponent. For example, a DerivedUnit of m/s^2 (acceleration) would have a factor of m^1 (meter with exponent 1) and s^-2 (second with exponent -2).</p><p>If the required UnitFactor is not found, then you need to create a new UnitFactor and use it to create new derived units.</p><p>To create a UnitFactor</p><hr /><ol><li>Create a new <strong>Instance Specification.</strong></li><li>Set its classifier to <strong>UnitFactor.</strong></li><li>Fill in the values of <strong>Unit </strong>and <strong>Exponent </strong>slots.</li></ol><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Derived Unit.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">An example of a derived unit(acceleration).</h6><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ddd1c26f-f1ce-4de3-a6af-ea31346b2ec5"><ac:rich-text-body><p>To learn more about derived units, refer to <a href="https://www.omg.org/spec/SysML">OMG SysML Specification</a>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/SYSMLP2024xR2/SysML+Block+Definition+Diagram">SysML Block Definition Diagram </a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR2/Instance+table">Instance Table</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR2/Package+import">Package import</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/SYSMLP2024xR2/Using+QUDV+model+library" rel="nofollow">Using QUDV model library</a></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Using Units" /></ac:link></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/SYSMLP2024xR2/Quantity+Kind">Quantity Kind</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/SYSMLP2024xR2/Value+Type">Value Type</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/SYSMLP2024xR2/Value+Property">Value Property</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/SYSMLP2024xR2/Block">Block</a></li></ul><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189146610 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00094: Decomposing Blocks

- page_id: `189146610`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189146610/Decomposing+Blocks
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-10-04T14:45:59.641+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > User Guide > Modeling structure with Blocks
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1021627255

1021627257

1187650235

**On this page**

**43**

1021627256

##### Introduction

You can decompose [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space='']by defining their specific features with properties. Block properties are used to capture the structural relationships and values of a Block. After the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Defining Blocks in Block Definition Diagram' space=''], you can decompose each Block into:

- Structural properties:
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Part Property' space=''] .
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Reference Property' space=''] .
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Value Property' space=''] .
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Constraint Property' space=''] .
  - Ports ( [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Flow Port' space=''] , [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Full Port' space=''] , [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Proxy Port' space=''] ).
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Flow Property' space=''] .
- Behavioral properties:
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operation' space='MD2024xR2'] .
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Reception' space='MD2024xR2'] .

In the figure below, you can see the *Computer*Block decomposed into four [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Part Property' space=''] and three [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Proxy Port' space='']. This decomposition can be shown directly in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space=''][CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML specific compartments' space=''](highlighted in blue), or it can be represented in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Internal Block Diagram' space=''] (highlighted in orange).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Computer Block decomposition and its representation in SysML Internal Block Diagram.

##### Creating and managing Block properties

You can create and manage [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space='']properties by using the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Smart manipulators' space='MD2024xR2'] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024xR2']. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating and managing Block properties' space='']>]]>

##### Creating SysML Internal Block Diagram

The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space='']properties are represented in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Internal Block Diagram' space='']. You can create this diagram directly for the selected Block shape. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating a SysML Internal Block diagram for a Block' space='']>]]>

If a selected Block already has properties, the opened [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying parts and ports' space=''] allows you to select which properties you want to represent.

The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Internal Block Diagram' space=''] can also be created in many other ways, as described in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating diagrams' space='MD2024xR2'] page.

##### Representing Block Properties in SysML Internal Block Diagram

You can represent Block properties in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Internal Block Diagram' space=''] by using the **Display Parts/Ports** dialog, diagram toolbar, the shortcut menu, smart manipulator, or drag-and-drop operation. All of these are described in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying parts and ports' space=''] page.

If you displayed nested properties, you can select the way you want to represent them. You can represent nested properties by displaying the property shape inside another property shape or by using dot notation. For detailed procedures how to work with property path notation, read the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Property path notation' space=''] page.

If properties have their own [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Internal Block Diagram' space=''], you can represent their internal structures inside shapes. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying internal structures on structured classifiers' space='']> ]]>

##### Selecting property types

The structural [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space='']properties must have a type that may be another Block or another concept, such as integer value. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Selecting property type' space='']>]]>

1021627254

**Related pages**

**Sample model**

The model used in the figure of this page is the **Inverted****Pendulum** sample model. To open this sample do one of the following:

- Download *[ATTACHMENT filename='InvertedPendulum.mdzip'].*
- Find in modeling tool <modeling tool installation directory>\samples\SysML\Inverted Pendulum\Inverted Pendulum.mdzip.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189146814 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00095: Default Rollup Pattern Blocks

- page_id: `189146814`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189146814/Default+Rollup+Pattern+Blocks
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-10-04T14:46:03.730+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > User Guide > Modeling structure with Blocks > Rollup Pattern Wizard
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1348391193

1348391196

1348391194

You can apply the default or[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating new Rollup Pattern Block' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] by using the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Rollup Pattern Wizard' space='SYSMLP2024xR2']**Rollup Pattern** **Wizard**. If you want to apply total cost, mass, or powercalculation for your model, you can use the default rollup patterns. The default rollup pattern Blocks are as follows:

- CostRollupPattern.
- MassRollupPattern.
- PowerRollupPattern.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The total cost, mass, or power rollup pattern Blocks defined in the SysML Block Definition Diagram.

The default rollup patterns are created to calculate the total cost, mass, and power of the model elements. Each pattern Block contains a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Parametric Diagram' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] that defines the calculation needs, and allows you to define any kind of rollup pattern. The patterns are created in association with a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Constraint Block' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] that defines the logic of the constraints. The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Constraint Property' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] type is a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Constraint Block' space='SYSMLP2024xR2']. The following figure demonstrates the Parametric Diagram of Cost Rollup Pattern.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The SysML Parametric Diagram of Cost Rollup Pattern.

All default rollup patterns are stored in each project under the auxiliary resources.The procedure below describes how to find them in your project.

To find default rollup pattern Blocks in a project

1. In the Containment tree, click [IMAGE alt='' src=''], and select the **Show Auxiliary Resources** option.
2. Select package *MD Customization for SysML* > *rollup patterns*.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The default rollup pattern Blocks are stored in the model under the auxiliary resources.

1348391192

**Related pages**

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Rollup Pattern Wizard' space='SYSMLP2024xR2']

**Webinar**

- Total Mass, Cost, and Power Rollups

**Sample model**

The sample model used in the figures of this page is the **Laptop Cost Analysis** that comes with [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation' space='CST2024xR2']. To open this sample do one of the following:

- Download *[ATTACHMENT filename='LaptopCostAnalysis.mdzip'].*
- Find in modeling tool <modeling tool installation directory>\samples\simulation\LaptopCostAnalysis.mdzip

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189148118 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00096: Default SysML values and instance specifications

- page_id: `189148118`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189148118/Default+SysML+values+and+instance+specifications
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2020-07-01T03:24:12.125+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > Exporting to External Simulation Models > Sample models > Hydraulics sample
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1. Three initial values required for the simulations are determined by instance specifications that are set in the fluidReservoir1, fluidReservoir2 and pipe part properties in the ConnectedTanks internal block diagram. Instance specifications t1 , t2 and p are assigned as the default values to each of these part properties fluidReservoir1 , fluidReservoir2 , pipe respectively. These specifications assign an initial values to the value properties that are constant throughout each simulation run and are required to solve the constraints.
2. The Tank block is a classifier of t1 and t2 instance specifications with default numeric values specified. The slots assigned default values are:
  1. **tanksufaceArea**: This slot determines the surface area of the tank, and is required to determine the amount of pressure in the tank as the fluid level changes. The unit is m2.
  2. **gravity**: This slot is the gravity constant assumed to be 9.8 m/s2. It is is also required to determine the amount of pressure in the tank as the fluid level changes.
  3. **fluidDensity**: This is a slot of the fluid in the tank itself that is coupled with and assigned to the tank. It is required for the constraints that determine the amount of fluid pressure in the tank. The unit for this property is kg/m3.
  4. fluidLevel : This slot determines the height of the fluid level in each tank at the start of the simulation but will change as the simulation runs and the fluid level height changes with the flow of fluid from one tank to the other. Their units are m.
3. The Pipe block is a classifier of p instance specification with default numeric values specified. The slots assigned default values are:
  1. pipeLength : This is a slot of the pipe that determines the resistance of the pipe to the flow of fluid through the pipe. The longer the pipe, the more resistance the fluid has to face to flow through the pipe. The unit is m.
  2. radius : Assuming the pipe has a circular cross-section, the radius is required to determine the cross-sectional area of the pipe. This is in turn required to determine the resistance of the pipe to the flow fluid through the pipe. The smaller the radius, the more resistance the fluid has to face to flow through the pipe. The unit is m.
  3. dynamicViscosity : This is a slot of the fluid in the pipe itself that is coupled with and assigned to the pipe. It is required for the constraints that determine the amount of resistance to fluid flow in the pipe. The unit for this property is kg/(s*m).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ol><li>Three initial values required for the simulations are determined by instance specifications that are set in the fluidReservoir1, fluidReservoir2 and <em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">pipe</em> part properties in the<em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> ConnectedTanks</em> internal block diagram. Instance specifications <em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">t1</em>, <em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">t2</em> and<em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> p</em> are assigned as the default values to each of these part properties <em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">fluidReservoir1</em>, <em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">fluidReservoir2</em>, <em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">pipe</em> respectively. These specifications assign an initial values to the value properties that are constant throughout each simulation run and are required to solve the constraints.</li><li>The <em>Tank</em> block is a classifier of t1 and t2 instance specifications with default numeric values specified. The slots assigned default values are:<ol><li><p><strong>tanksufaceArea</strong>: This slot determines the surface area of the tank, and is required to determine the amount of pressure in the tank as the fluid level changes. The unit is m2.</p></li><li><p><strong>gravity</strong>: This slot is the gravity constant assumed to be 9.8 m/s2. It is is also required to determine the amount of pressure in the tank as the fluid level changes.</p></li><li><p><strong>fluidDensity</strong>: This is a slot of the fluid in the tank itself that is coupled with and assigned to the tank. It is required for the constraints that determine the amount of fluid pressure in the tank. The unit for this property is kg/m3.</p></li><li><strong>fluidLevel</strong>: This slot determines the height of the fluid level in each tank at the start of the simulation but will change as the simulation runs and the fluid level height changes with the flow of fluid from one tank to the other. Their units are m.</li></ol></li><li>The <em>Pipe</em> block is a classifier of <em>p</em> instance specification with default numeric values specified. The slots assigned default values are:<ol><li><strong>pipeLength</strong>: This is a slot of the pipe that determines the resistance of the pipe to the flow of fluid through the pipe. The longer the pipe, the more resistance the fluid has to face to flow through the pipe. The unit is m.</li><li><strong>radius</strong>: Assuming the pipe has a circular cross-section, the radius is required to determine the cross-sectional area of the pipe. This is in turn required to determine the resistance of the pipe to the flow fluid through the pipe. The smaller the radius, the more resistance the fluid has to face to flow through the pipe. The unit is m.</li><li><strong>dynamicViscosity</strong>: This is a slot of the fluid in the pipe itself that is coupled with and assigned to the pipe. It is required for the constraints that determine the amount of resistance to fluid flow in the pipe. The unit for this property is kg/(s*m).</li></ol></li></ol><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="containtment_tree_hydraulics_model.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Default SysML values and instance specifications" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p />
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189148170 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00097: Default SysML values and specifications of the instance

- page_id: `189148170`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189148170/Default+SysML+values+and+specifications+of+the+instance
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2020-07-01T02:37:33.365+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > Exporting to External Simulation Models > Sample models > Signal processor sample
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1. The *SignalSource*, *Amplifier,**HighPassFilter*, and*LowPassFilter* blocks are classifiers of *ss*, *ampfr*, *hpfvar* and *lpfvar* instance specifications. Each have a slot with numeric value defined that stays constant throughout each simulation run and are required to solve the constraints. The value of slot *alfa* in each of the *hpfvar* and *lpfvar*instance specifications are required to determine the cut-off frequency for the signal filters. The value of slot *g*in *ampfr* instance specification**is required to determine the gain factor of the *Amplifier* block. The value of slot *amp* in the ss instance specification determines the amplitude of the generated signal.
2. Three initial values required for the simulations are determined by instance specifications that are set in the two filter part properties and amplifier part property (parts *hPF*, *lPF* and*a*) in the *SignalProcessor* internal block diagram. Instance specifications *hpfvar*, *lpfvar*and*ampfr*are assigned as the default values to each of these part properties *hPF*, *lPF* and*a,* respectively. These specifications assign an initial value for the unit-less variable*x* in each part that is required for filtering the signal in the signal processor at the start of the simulation. But as the simulation runs, these values do change. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ol><li><p>The <em>SignalSource</em>, <em>Amplifier, </em><em>HighPassFilter</em>, and<em> LowPassFilter</em> blocks are classifiers of <em>ss</em>, <em>ampfr</em>, <em>hpfvar</em> and <em>lpfvar</em> instance specifications. Each have a slot with numeric value defined that stays constant throughout each simulation run and are required to solve the constraints. The value of slot <em>alfa</em> in each of the <em>hpfvar</em> and <em>lpfvar </em>instance specifications are required to determine the cut-off frequency for the signal filters. The value of slot <em>g </em>in <em>ampfr</em> instance specification<em> </em>is required to determine the gain factor of the <em>Amplifier</em> block. The value of slot <em>amp</em> in the ss instance specification determines the amplitude of the generated signal.</p></li><li><p>Three initial values required for the simulations are determined by instance specifications that are set in the two filter part properties and amplifier part property (parts <em>hPF</em>, <em>lPF</em> and<em> a</em>) in the <em>SignalProcessor</em> internal block diagram. Instance specifications <em>hpfvar</em>, <em>lpfvar </em>and<em> ampfr  </em>are assigned as the default values to each of these part properties <em>hPF</em>, <em>lPF</em> and<em> a,</em> respectively. These specifications assign an initial value for the unit-less variable<em> x</em> in each part that is required for filtering the signal in the signal processor at the start of the simulation. But as the simulation runs, these values do change.</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="containment_tree_signal_model.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Default SysML values and specifications of the instance" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></li></ol><p />
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189146602 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00098: Defining Blocks in Block Definition Diagram

- page_id: `189146602`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189146602/Defining+Blocks+in+Block+Definition+Diagram
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-10-04T14:45:59.484+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > User Guide > Modeling structure with Blocks
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1020463451

1020463453

1092035030

**On this page**

43

1020463452

##### Introduction

When describing your system structure, you should start by defining [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] in a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Block Definition Diagram' space='SYSMLP2024xR2']. Blocks represent the system hierarchy in terms of systems and subsystems. You can model either the logical or physical decomposition of a system, and the specification of software, hardware, or human elements.

The notation for a Block is a rectangle with the stereotype «block» preceding the name. Blocks are the elements of definition because they have a name only.The figure below illustrates the vehicle structure ofdefined Blocks in the SysML Block Definition Diagram. The figure is created from the [ATTACHMENT filename='VehicleStructure.mdzip'] sample model.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The vehicle structure in a SysML Block Definition Diagram.

##### Creating Blocks

You can create [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] in a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Block Definition Diagram' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] in the following ways:

44

###### Use the Create Element dialog in the Model Browser

You can create all elements including [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model Browser' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] from the shortcut menu.

To create a Block in the Model Browser

1. In the Model Browser, right-click the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Package' space='CBMP2024xR2'] in which you want to store the new Block.
2. Do one of the following: - From the shortcut menu, select Create Element . - Press Ctrl+Shift+E.
3. In the Create Element dialog, select Block.
4. Type its name. The new Block is created in the Model Browser. You can represent it in the SysML Block Definition Diagram by dragging it from the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model Browser' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] onto the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding the user interface' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] .

###### Use the palette on the diagram pane

You can create [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] directly on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Block Definition Diagram' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] pane. The Blocks created on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding the user interface' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] are created in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model Browser' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'], too.

To create a Block on the diagram pane

1. Open the existing SysML Block Definition Diagram or create a new one. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating a diagram' space='SYSMLP2024xR2']>]]>
2. Do one of the following: - From the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding the user interface' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] , select the Block button. - Press B.
3. Click on the diagram pane.
4. Type its name. The new Block is created in the Model Browser and represented in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Block Definition Diagram' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] .

###### Paste a list in the Containment tree or on a diagram pane

You can create Blocks by copying a listed text from other resources (e.g. Word, Excel, HTML, etc.) and pasting it either in the Containment tree or diagram pane.

Detailed procedures are provided in the [Creating elements from other resources](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/SYSMLP2024xR2/Creating+elements+from+other+resources) page.

###### Drag .fmu file on the diagram pane

You can represent any model components that are exported to Functional Mock-up Interface (FMI) standard (.fmu files) as Blocks in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Block Definition Diagram' space='SYSMLP190'].

The SysML Plugin supports FMI 1.0 and 2.0 versions.

To represent an .fmu file as the Block

1. Drag the .fmu file onto the Block Definition Diagram pane. The Block with name, value properties, their types, and applied fmu stereotype is created. [ATTACHMENT filename='drag_fmu_file.png']

You can execute one fmu Block or co-simulate cooperated fmu Blocks using the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation' space='CST2024xR2'].

##### Creating Association Block

You can use an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Association Block' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] to show decomposition of the connector similar to the way that blocks show the decomposition of parts. You can create an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Association Block' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Association Block with Owned Ends' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] between two [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Block Definition Diagram' space='SYSMLP2024xR2']. You can represent Association Block usages in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Internal Block Diagram' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] by [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using Association Block as Connector type' space='SYSMLP2024xR2']setting Association Block as Connector type.

To create an Association Block or Association Block with Owned Ends

1. Create two Blocks on a diagram pane.
2. From the diagram palette, select Association Block or Association Block with Owned Ends button.
3. On the diagram pane, select a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] to be used as the source.
4. Select a target by either selecting an existing Block on the diagram pane, or by clicking on empty space on the diagram to create the target Block. An Association Block or Association Block with Owned Ends is created between the source and target [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] . The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Participant Property' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] are created for the Association Block automatically and shown in its references [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML specific compartments' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] area.

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Participant Property' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] are created automatically after an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Association Block' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] is created between [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'].

The following figure shows two Association Blocks: *Water Delivery* and *Plumbing*. The connection between the *Spigot Bank* Block and *Faucet* Blocks is decomposed into the *Water Delivery* Block by creating an Association Block. The suppliedByInLink and deliveredToInLink Participant Properties are created and shown in the compartment area. The same is shown with the *Plumbling* Association Block. The figure is created from the [ATTACHMENT filename='WaterSupply_19.0.mdzip']sample model.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']Water Delivery and Plumbing Association Blocks in the SysML Block Definition Diagram.

##### Relating Blocks

You can connect Blocks by using these main relationships:

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Defining Blocks in Block Definition Diagram' space='SYSMLP2024xR2']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Defining Blocks in Block Definition Diagram' space='SYSMLP2024xR2']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Defining Blocks in Block Definition Diagram' space='SYSMLP2024xR2']

**genGeneralization**

The Generalization relationship conveys an inheritance between Blocks. It is usually used to create a hierarchy in your system. The notation is a solid line with a hollow, triangular arrowhead on the end.

The figure below shows that the *DC Power Supply*, *Inverted Pendulum System*, and *Computer* Blocks are types of the *AC Device* Block. All of the subtypes (*DC Power Supply*, *Inverted Pendulum System*, and *Computer*) require all the characteristics of *AC Device* but add their own specialized characteristics as well. The figure is created from the [ATTACHMENT filename='InvertedPendulum.mdzip'] sample model. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Generalization relationship between Blocks in SysML Block Definition Diagram.

**compDirect Composition or Composition**

The Direct Composition or Composition relationships convey a structural decomposition of Blocks. The notation is a solid line between two Blocks with a solid diamond on the composite end.

The following figure shows that the *Wheel* is composed of *Tire* and *Brake*. The Composition can be represented in two different ways: by showing Composition relations between Blocksor by showing Part Properties on a decomposed Block [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML specific compartments' space='SYSMLP2024xR2']. The figure is created from the [ATTACHMENT filename='VehicleStructure.mdzip'] sample model. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Direct Composition relationship between Blocks in the SysML Block Definition Diagram.

**assocDirect Association or Association**

The Direct Association or Association conveysthat a connection can exist between those Blocks. Those Blocks can access each other for some purpose across the connection. The Direct Association notation isa solid line with an arrowhead on the end, while the Association notation consists only of asolid line.

The figure below illustrates the Association between the*Wheel* and *WirelessTirePressureMonitor* Blocks. The name of the Association is *BandMount,* which describes the type of connection that could exist between the wheel and wireless tire pressure monitor*.* The Association can be represented in two different ways: by showing the Association relation between Blocks or by showing the Reference Property on a decomposed Block [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML specific compartments' space='SYSMLP2024xR2']. The figure**is created from the [ATTACHMENT filename='hybrid sport utility vehicle.mdzip'] sample model.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Association relationship between Blocks in SysML Block Definition Diagram.

For how to create, remove, change a style, route, create a line jump, insert a shape on a path, or manage path compartments, read the [Working with paths and relationships](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR2/Working+with+paths+and+relationships) page.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ed7e1079-8d84-4a26-a375-596251dc17c1"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1020463451</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c6af0100-ecc2-4233-9fa9-643be021db7c"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1020463453</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="20da96c7-7dda-4dbb-aab6-4826309fb069"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1092035030</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="73952ed5-e5f4-4c14-bee9-79bd8b0adea5"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">4</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="minLevel">3</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d70ab6ed-9a36-4520-a9fe-cf2e8034dfeb"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1020463452</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><h3>Introduction</h3><p>When describing your system structure, you should start by defining <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Block" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Blocks]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> in a <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML Block Definition Diagram" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[SysML Block Definition Diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. Blocks <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">represent the system hierarchy in terms of systems and subsystems. You can <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">model either the logical or physical decomposition of a system, and the specification of software, hardware, or human elements.</span></span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">The notation for a Block is a rectangle with the stereotype «block» preceding the name. <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">Blocks are the elements of definition because they have a name only. </span>T<span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">he figure below illustrates the vehicle structure of </span>defined Blocks in the SysML Block Definition Diagram. The figure is created from the <ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="VehicleStructure.mdzip"><ri:page ri:content-title="_sample models" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:link> sample model.</span></span></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="vehicleStructure.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Defining Blocks in Block Definition Diagram" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The vehicle structure in a SysML Block Definition Diagram.</h6><h3><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">Creating Blocks</span></h3><p>You can create <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Block" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Blocks]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> in a <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML Block Definition Diagram" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[SysML Block Definition Diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> in the following ways:</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3852d11a-45ba-4865-bbe7-83bd240a9640"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">4</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="minLevel">4</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p><h4>Use the Create Element dialog in the Model Browser</h4><p>You can create all elements including <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Block" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Blocks]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Model Browser" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ac:link> from the shortcut menu.</p><p>To create a Block in the Model Browser</p><hr /><ol><li>In the Model Browser, right-click the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CBMP2024xR2" ri:content-title="Package" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Package ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>in which you want to store the new Block.</li><li>Do one of the following:<br />   - From the shortcut menu, select <strong>Create Element</strong>.<br />   - Press Ctrl+Shift+E.</li><li>In the <strong>Create Element</strong> dialog, select Block.</li><li>Type its name.<br />The new Block is created in the Model Browser. You can represent it in the SysML Block Definition Diagram by dragging it from the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Model Browser" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ac:link> onto the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Understanding the user interface" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[diagram pane]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li></ol><h4>Use the palette on the diagram pane</h4><p>You can create <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Block" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Blocks]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> directly on the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML Block Definition Diagram" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ac:link> pane. The Blocks created on the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Understanding the user interface" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[diagram pane]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> are created in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Model Browser" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ac:link>, too.</p><p><br /></p><p>To create a Block on the diagram pane</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the existing SysML Block Definition Diagram or create a new one. <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating a diagram" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[How to create a new diagram >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li>Do one of the following:<br />  - From the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Understanding the user interface" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[diagram palette]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, select the <strong>Block</strong> button.<br />  - Press B.</li><li>Click on the diagram pane.</li><li>Type its name.<br />The new Block is created in the Model Browser and represented in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML Block Definition Diagram" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ac:link>.</li></ol><h4>Paste a list in the Containment tree or on a diagram pane</h4><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">You can create Blocks by copying a listed text from other resources (e.g. Word, Excel, HTML, etc.) and pasting it either in the Containment tree or diagram pane. </span></p><p>Detailed procedures are provided in the <a class="current" href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/SYSMLP2024xR2/Creating+elements+from+other+resources">Creating elements from other resources</a> page.</p><h4>Drag <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">.fmu file on the diagram pane</span></h4><p>You can represent any model components that are exported to Functional Mock-up Interface (FMI) standard (.fmu files) as Blocks in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="SYSMLP190" ri:content-title="SysML Block Definition Diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[SysML Block Definition Diagrams]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="97b9590c-3e7c-4bed-9d4d-d3219ede20fb"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The SysML Plugin supports FMI 1.0 and 2.0 versions.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br />To represent an .fmu file as the Block</p><hr /><ol><li>Drag the .fmu file onto the Block Definition Diagram pane.<br />The Block with name, value properties, their types, and applied fmu stereotype is created.<br /><ac:image ac:title="Dragging FMU file on Block Definition Diagram pane" ac:alt="Dragging FMU file on Block Definition Diagram pane"><ri:attachment ri:filename="drag_fmu_file.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Defining Blocks in Block Definition Diagram" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><p><br /></p><p>You can execute one fmu Block or co-simulate cooperated fmu Blocks using the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024xR2" ri:content-title="Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Cameo Simulation Toolkit]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><p><br /></p><h3>Creating Association Block</h3><p>You can use an <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Association Block" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ac:link> to show decomposition of the connector similar to the way that blocks show the decomposition of parts. You can create an <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Association Block" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ac:link> or <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Association Block with Owned Ends" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ac:link> between two <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Block" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Blocks]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML Block Definition Diagram" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ac:link>. You can represent Association Block usages in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML Internal Block Diagram" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[SysML Internal Block Diagrams]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> by <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Using Association Block as Connector type" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /><ac:link-body>setting <span class="current">Association Block as Connector type</span></ac:link-body></ac:link><span class="current">.</span></p><p>To create an Association Block or Association Block with Owned Ends</p><hr /><ol><li>Create two Blocks on a diagram pane.</li><li>From the diagram palette, select <strong>Association Block </strong>or <strong>Association Block with Owned Ends</strong> button.</li><li>On the diagram pane, select a <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Block" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ac:link> to be used as the source.</li><li>Select a target by either selecting an existing Block on the diagram pane, or by clicking on empty space on the diagram to create the target Block.<br />An Association Block or Association Block with Owned Ends is created between the source and target <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Block" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Blocks]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. The <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Participant Property" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Participant Properties]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> are created for the Association Block automatically and shown in its <em>references</em> <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML specific compartments" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[compartment ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>area.</li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0cc59ef3-68ec-4b55-ba00-58d27118c434"><ac:rich-text-body><p><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Participant Property" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Participant properties]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> are created automatically after an <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Association Block" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ac:link> is created between <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Block" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Blocks]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The following figure shows two Association Blocks: <em>Water Delivery</em> and <em>Plumbing</em>. The connection between the <em>Spigot Bank</em> Block and <em>Faucet</em> Blocks is decomposed into the <em>Water Delivery</em> Block by creating an Association Block. The suppliedByInLink and deliveredToInLink Participant Properties are created and shown in the compartment area. The same is shown with the <em>Plumbling</em> Association Block. <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">The figure is created from the <ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="WaterSupply_19.0.mdzip"><ri:page ri:content-title="Using Association Block as Connector type" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:link></span><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> sample model.</span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="association_block.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Defining Blocks in Block Definition Diagram" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>Water Delivery and Plumbing Association Blocks in the SysML Block Definition Diagram.</h6><h3>Relating Blocks</h3><p>You can connect Blocks by using these main relationships:</p><ul><li><ac:link ac:anchor="gen"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Generalization]]></ac:plain-text-link-body><ri:page ri:content-title="Defining Blocks in Block Definition Diagram" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link ac:anchor="comp"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Direct Composition or Composition]]></ac:plain-text-link-body><ri:page ri:content-title="Defining Blocks in Block Definition Diagram" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link ac:anchor="assoc"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Direct Association or Association]]></ac:plain-text-link-body><ri:page ri:content-title="Defining Blocks in Block Definition Diagram" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ac:link><strong><br /></strong></li></ul><p><br /></p><p><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c401d354-28d1-42da-ba33-c036bbb91f64"><ac:parameter ac:name="">gen</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>Generalization</strong></p><p>The Generalization relationship conveys an inheritance between Blocks. It is usually used to create a hierarchy in your system. The notation is a solid line with a hollow, triangular arrowhead on the end.</p><p>The figure below shows that the <em>DC Power Supply</em>, <em>Inverted Pendulum System</em>, and <em>Computer</em> Blocks are types of the <em>AC Device</em> Block. All of the subtypes (<em>DC Power Supply</em>, <em>Inverted Pendulum System</em>, and <em>Computer</em>) require all the characteristics of <em>AC Device</em> but add their own specialized characteristics as well. The figure is created from the <ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="InvertedPendulum.mdzip"><ri:page ri:content-title="_sample models" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:link> sample model. <br /><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="generalization_relationship.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Defining Blocks in Block Definition Diagram" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Generalization relationship between Blocks in SysML Block Definition Diagram.</h6><p><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1d25a63e-1920-4a0d-841b-a8f28efb2f53"><ac:parameter ac:name="">comp</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>Direct Composition or Composition</strong></p><p>The Direct Composition or Composition relationships convey a structural decomposition of Blocks. The notation is a solid line between two Blocks with a solid diamond on the composite end.</p><p>The following figure shows that the <em>Wheel</em> is composed of <em>Tire</em> and <em>Brake</em>. The Composition can be represented in two different ways: by showing Composition relations <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">between Blocks </span>or by showing Part Properties on a decomposed Block <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML specific compartments" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[compartment]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. The figure is created from the <ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="VehicleStructure.mdzip"><ri:page ri:content-title="_sample models" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:link> sample model.<br /><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="composition_relationship.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Defining Blocks in Block Definition Diagram" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span style="color: rgb(112,112,112);">Direct Composition relationship between Blocks in the SysML Block Definition Diagram.</span></h6><p><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="11ef6e36-5f30-49dd-b898-43b8f534276d"><ac:parameter ac:name="">assoc</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>Direct Association or Association</strong></p><p>The Direct Association or Association conveys<span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> that a connection can exist between those Blocks.</span> Those Blocks can access each other for some purpose across the connection. The Direct Association notation is<span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> a solid line with an arrowhead on the end, while the Association notation consists only of a<span> solid line. </span></span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">The figure below illustrates the Association between the </span><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><em>Wheel</em> and <em>WirelessTirePressureMonitor</em> Blocks. The name of the Association is <em>BandMount,</em> which describes the type of connection that could exist between the wheel and wireless tire pressure monitor<em>.</em> The Association can be represented in two different ways: by showing the Association relation between Blocks or by showing the Reference Property on a decomposed Block <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML specific compartments" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[compartment]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. The figure<em> </em>is created from the <ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="hybrid sport utility vehicle.mdzip"><ri:page ri:content-title="_sample models" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:link> sample model. </span></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="association_relationship.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Defining Blocks in Block Definition Diagram" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Association relationship between Blocks in SysML Block Definition Diagram.</h6><p><br /></p><p>For how to create, remove, change a style, route, create a line jump, insert a shape on a path, or manage path compartments, read the <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR2/Working+with+paths+and+relationships" class="current">Working with paths and relationships</a> page.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189146959 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00099: Defining Constraint Blocks

- page_id: `189146959`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189146959/Defining+Constraint+Blocks
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2018-04-20T13:20:20.609+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > User Guide > Modeling parametric constraints
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1321284049

1321284051

1321284050

With a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Constraint Block' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'], you can define parameters needed to calculate a formula. In a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Parametric Diagram' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'], Constraint Blocks are used as types of [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Constraint Property' space='SYSMLP2024xR2']. You can use the same Constraint Block in several Contexts. Usually, constraints are stored in a library, where you will be able to export and reuse them in other models.

To create a Constraint Block

1. Open a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Block Definition Diagram' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating a diagram' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] .
2. Click the Constraint Block button on the diagram palette, then click an empty space on the diagram pane. An unnamed constraint block is created.
3. Type a Constraint Block name directly on the shape of that element and press Enter.
4. In a constraints compartment, type a formula.
5. Create parameters. Click the Constraint Block. The smart manipulator appears. Click the Parse and Create Parameters . [ATTACHMENT filename='parse_and_create_parameters.png']
6. According to the formula, parameters having default types assigned are created. If necessary, you may change the type of the particular parameter. [ATTACHMENT filename='constraint_block.png']

1321284047

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Block Definition Diagram' space='SYSMLP2024xR2']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying parameters and properties' space='SYSMLP2024xR2']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space='SYSMLP2024xR2']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="55c0405b-762d-44e0-a05e-1c919881f889"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1321284049</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4be599f9-c8fe-4500-8ac1-ac61273b287a"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1321284051</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="412d76fd-5d20-4388-83e4-581ef19353eb"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1321284050</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>With a <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Constraint Block" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ac:link>, you can define parameters needed to calculate a formula. In a <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML Parametric Diagram" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Parametric diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, Constraint Blocks are used as types of <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Constraint Property" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Constraint Properties]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. You can use the same Constraint Block in several Contexts. Usually, constraints are stored in a library, where you will be able to export and reuse them in other models.</p><p><br /></p><p>To create a Constraint Block</p><hr /><ol><li>Open a <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML Block Definition Diagram" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Block Definition Diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> or <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating a diagram" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[create a new one]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li><li>Click the <strong>Constraint Block</strong> button on the diagram palette, then click an empty space on the diagram pane. An unnamed constraint block is created.</li><li>Type a Constraint Block name <span>directly </span><span>on the shape of that element and press Enter.</span></li><li>In a constraints compartment, type a formula.</li><li>Create parameters. Click the Constraint Block. The smart manipulator appears. Click the <strong>Parse and Create Parameters</strong>.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="parse_and_create_parameters.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Defining Constraint Blocks" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>According to the formula, parameters having default types assigned are created. If necessary, you may change the type of the particular parameter.<br /><ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="149"><ri:attachment ri:filename="constraint_block.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Defining Constraint Blocks" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3d48a97b-db15-4dd9-947f-470dd1dbd714"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1321284047</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML Block Definition Diagram" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Displaying parameters and properties" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Block" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189146693 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00102: Defining Interface Blocks

- page_id: `189146693`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189146693/Defining+Interface+Blocks
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-10-04T14:46:01.239+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > User Guide > Modeling structure with Blocks > Defining interfaces using ports
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1332798826

1332798829

1332798827

To define Interface Block

1. Create Interface Block in the model in one of the following way:
  - F rom the[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Block Definition Diagram' space=''] [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding the user interface' space=''], select the**Interface Block** button, and click the on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding the user interface' space=''].
  - In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model Browser' space=''], right-click the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Package' space='MD2024xR2']in which you want to store your interfaces, and select **Create Element** > **Interface Block**.
2. Specify Flow Properties in one of the following way:
  - Open the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024xR2'] of Interface Block, select Properties group, click the Create button, and select Flow Property. Type its name, specify its direction, other properties, and click Close .
  - In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model Browser' space=''], right-click the Interface Block, and select **Create Element** > **Flow Property**. Type its name, specify its direction, or other properties.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Defined Interface Block and specified its Flow Properties in the Model Browser and Specification window.

1332798825

**Related pages**

**Sample model**

The model used in the figure of this page is the **Inverted****Pendulum** sample model. To open this sample do one of the following:

- Download *[InvertedPendulum.mdzip](https://docs.nomagic.com/download/attachments/9919520/InvertedPendulum.mdzip?version=1&modificationDate=1498477335401&api=v2).*
- Find in modeling tool <modeling tool installation directory>\samples\SysML\Inverted Pendulum\Inverted Pendulum.mdzip.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189146670 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00103: Defining interfaces using ports

- page_id: `189146670`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189146670/Defining+interfaces+using+ports
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-10-04T14:46:00.711+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > User Guide > Modeling structure with Blocks
- labels: ['conjugate', 'is_conjugated', 'interface', 'ports']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1021730330

1021730332

1021730329

Ports are points at which external entities can connect to and interact with a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space=''] in different or more limited ways than connecting directly to the Block itself. They are properties with a type that specifies features available to the external entities via [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Connector' space='MD2024xR2'] to the ports. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Proxy Port' space=''] define the boundary by specifying which features of the owning [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space=''] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Part Property' space=''] are visible through external connectors, while [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Full Port' space=''] define the boundary with their own features. Proxy Ports are always typed by [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Interface Block' space=''], a specialized kind of [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space=''] that has no behaviors or internal parts. Full Ports cannot be behavioral in the UML sense of standing in for the owning object, because they handle features themselves, rather than exposing features of their owners, or internal parts of their owners.

In the following figure, the *chanel2* Proxy Port is typed by *GrayCodeIB* Interface Block which contains two Flow Properties of *Pulse* Signal:*out a* and *out b*. It means the Proxy Port that is typed by *GrayCodeIB* Interface Block can send and receive Flow Items of *Pulse* Signal.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The GrayCodeIB Interface Block is a type of Proxy Ports.

To define interfaces using ports

1. Create a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space=''] and decompose it in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Internal Block Diagram' space=''] . [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Defining Blocks in Block Definition Diagram' space='']>]]> [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Decomposing Blocks' space='']>]]>
2. In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Internal Block Diagram' space='']SysML Internal Block Diagram , create [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Proxy Port' space=''] . [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating ports' space='']>]]>
3. Create [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Interface Block' space=''] in the model and specify its [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Flow Property' space=''] . [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Defining Interface Blocks' space='']How to define Interface Block >>
4. Select create Interface Block as Proxy Port type. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Selecting port type' space='']>]]>

If ports are connected via [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Connector' space='MD2024xR2'], both port types must be compatible.

If the same [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Interface Block' space=''] is set as the type for ports on both ends, one of the ports should be conjugated. The conjugate mechanism reverses the direction of the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Flow Property' space=''] and makes the connection compatible. For this, you can conjugate a port or create a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Conjugated Interface Block' space=''].

To conjugate a port, do one of the following:

- On the diagram pane, right-click the port shape, and select the Is Conjugated command.
- Open the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024xR2'] of port, and set the Is Conjugated property value to true. The port is conjugated and the tilde symbol (~) appears before the port's type. See the following figure. [ATTACHMENT filename='conjugated_port.png']

To create a Conjugated Interface Block

- Right-click one or more Interface Blocks and select Tools > Create Conjugated Interface Block . The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Conjugated Interface Block' space=''] is created with the same [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Directed Feature' space=''] and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Flow Property' space=''] as the original but with opposite directions. It is created under the same owner as the original Interface Block.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

1180121346

**Related pages**

**Sample model**

The model used in the figure of this page is the **Inverted****Pendulum** sample model. To open this sample do one of the following:

- Download *[ATTACHMENT filename='InvertedPendulum.mdzip'].*
- Find in modeling tool <modeling tool installation directory>\samples\SysML\Inverted Pendulum\Inverted Pendulum.mdzip.

to create Block internal structure >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li>In the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML Internal Block Diagram" /><ac:link-body>SysML <span /></ac:link>, both port types must be compatible.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p>If the same <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Interface Block" /></ac:link> is set as the type for ports on both ends, one of the ports should be conjugated. The conjugate mechanism reverses the direction of the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Flow Property" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Flow Properties]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> and makes the connection compatible.
