Click <strong>Next</strong>.</li><li>In the <strong>Select Report Data</strong> area, you can then select a predefined report data for the selected template (default = Built-in). 564248573

564248572

The SysML plugin retains all capabilities of modeling tool architecture modeling environment with System Engineer perspective. With this plugin, modeling tool adds support for additional specification, analysis, design, and validation of a broad range of systems and system integrations.

How to start:****

564248570

**SysML sample models**

The SysML sample projects are available in the ***<md.install.dir>/samples/SysML*** directory.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189146895 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00163: Handling inconsistencies between structure and behavior models

- page_id: `189146895`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189146895/Handling+inconsistencies+between+structure+and+behavior+models
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-03-21T12:57:05.207+01:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > User Guide > Behavior to structure synchronization
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Tip

464453088

INLINE

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Tip

464453099

INLINE

464453086

INLINE

**On this page**

**3**

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Tip

464453089

INLINE

Three validation rules complement each other to check whether the structure model is compatible with the behavior model. If any inconsistencies are found, the structure model can be automatically synchronized with the behavior model using solvers. First, you need to synchronize interfaces so that you can handle Connectors and, finally, Item Flows. As a consequence, the manual work is reduced.

#### TIP: Tip

Tip

You can handle detected inconsistencies using solvers directly in:

- Internal Block Diagram
- Validation Results panel

##### Handling missing Proxy Ports and Interface Blocks

The first validation rule checks whether Proxy Ports and Interface Blocks are compatible with flows that flow between the two different Part Properties in Activities. The missing Proxy Ports and Interface Blocks can be handled automatically using solvers.

To create missing Proxy Ports and Interface Blocks

1. Do one of the following:
  - In the Internal Block Diagram, select a Part Property highlighted in yellow, and then in the smart manipulator toolbar, click [ATTACHMENT filename='triangle_symbol_smart_manipulator_toolbar.png'] . [IMAGE alt='' src='']
  - In the open **Validation Results** panel, right-click a validation result. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Select one of the following:

1. 
  - Create Port - creates a new Proxy Port typed by a compatible Interface Block.
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Handling inconsistencies between structure and behavior models' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] - changes an already existing Proxy Port type to a compatible Interface Block.
  - Add Missing Flow Property - adds a new Flow Property to an Interface Block that types a Proxy Port.
  - Reverse Direction of Port - changes the Proxy Port direction, thus changing the Flow Property direction of an Interface Block that types a Proxy Port.

A Proxy Port typed by a compatible Interface Block is created.

###### Choosing a compatible Interface Block

If you select the **Choose Compatible Interface Block** solver, the **Choose Proxy Port with Compatible Interface Block**dialog opens.

You can now choose a Proxy Port and type it with an Interface Block that has Flow Properties compatible with the type and direction of the missing flow.

Alternatively, you can create a new Proxy Port and an Interface Block to type the Proxy Port. For this, select **<NEW>**.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Choosing a Proxy Port and specifying a compatible Interface Block as its type.

##### Handling missing Connectors

The second validation rule checks whether there is a Connector between the two compatible Proxy Ports owned by the Part Property. The missing Connectors can be handled automatically using solvers.

To create missing Connectors

1. Do one of the following:
  - In the Internal Block Diagram, select a Proxy Port highlighted in yellow, and then in the smart manipulator toolbar, click [ATTACHMENT filename='triangle_symbol_smart_manipulator_toolbar.png'] . [ATTACHMENT filename='btos_second_validation_rule.png']
  - In the open Validation Results panel, right-click a validation result. [ATTACHMENT filename='second_validation_rule_creating_connector_from_validation_results_panel.png']
2. Select Create Connector .

A Connector between the two compatible Proxy Ports is created.

##### Handling missing Item Flows

The third validation rule checks realized Item Flows in Activities and helps to ensure the correct Item Flows are realized on Connectors that connect Proxy Ports typed by compatible Interface Blocks. The missing Item Flows can be handled automatically using solvers.

To realize missing Item Flows on Connectors

1. Do one of the following:
  - In the Internal Block Diagram, select a Connector highlighted in yellow, and then in the smart manipulator toolbar, click [ATTACHMENT filename='triangle_symbol_smart_manipulator_toolbar.png'] . [ATTACHMENT filename='third_btos_validation_rule_IBD.png']
  - In the open Validation Results panel, right-click a validation result. [ATTACHMENT filename='third_btos_validation_rule_validation_results_panel.png']
2. Select one of the following:
  - Realize Missing Item Flow - realizes the compatible existing Item Flow on the Connector that was found in the behavior model.
  - Open Item Flow Manager - opens the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Item Flow Manager dialog' space='SYSMLP2024xR2'] dialog.
  - Change Item Flow Realization Compatibility Checking - opens the Project Options dialog to enable/disable the Item Flow realization checking.

A compatible Item Flow is realized on the selected Connector.

##### Navigating to elements

To learn more about the validated elements, in the smart manipulator toolbar of the Internal Block Diagram or Validation Results panel, select the **Navigate To** command to navigate to:

- Activity Edges in the Activity diagram from which the missing flow has been identified.
- Part Properties in the Internal Block Diagram between which items are flowing.

Elements are automatically selected on the diagram pane and in the Containment tree.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Navigating to an element from the smart manipulator toolbar.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="960074de-fd04-4de9-bb65-6fffaaf7e10e"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">464453088</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="06d83478-7b74-4a66-907e-56899d0f26b6"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">464453099</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="628463d5-cf73-48eb-9fa5-9bec2d0c87a5"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">464453086</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ad388396-2752-401a-824f-40a2fa1b96e8"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">3</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro><br /></strong></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="44f990f7-c522-456b-b4a8-a9b098afcd74"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">464453089</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Three validation rules complement each other to check whether the structure model is compatible with the behavior model. If any inconsistencies are found, the structure model can be automatically synchronized with the behavior model using solvers. missing Proxy Ports and Interface Blocks</p><hr /><ol><li>Do one of the following:<ul><li>In the Internal Block Diagram, select a Part Property highlighted in yellow, and then in the smart manipulator toolbar, click <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="triangle_symbol_smart_manipulator_toolbar.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Handling inconsistencies between structure and behavior models" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><span>.</span><br /><span><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="behavior_to_structure_solvers_on_IBD.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Handling inconsistencies between structure and behavior models" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /></span></li><li><span>In the open <strong>Validation Results</strong> panel, right-click a validation result. </span><br /><span><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="btos_validation_results_panel.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Handling inconsistencies between structure and behavior models" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /></span></li></ul></li><li>Select one of the following:</li></ol><ol><li style="list-style-type: none;background-image: none;"><ul><li><strong>Create Port </strong>- creates a new Proxy Port typed by a compatible Interface Block.</li><li><strong><ac:link ac:anchor="Choosing a compatible Interface Block"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Choose Compatible Interface Block]]></ac:plain-text-link-body><ri:page ri:content-title="Handling inconsistencies between structure and behavior models" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ac:link> </strong>- changes an already existing Proxy Port type to a compatible Interface Block.<strong><br /></strong></li><li><strong>Add Missing Flow Property </strong>- adds a new Flow Property to an Interface Block that types a Proxy Port.</li><li><strong>Reverse Direction of Port </strong>- changes the Proxy Port direction, thus changing the Flow Property direction of an Interface Block that types a Proxy Port.</li></ul></li></ol><p>A Proxy Port typed by a compatible Interface Block is created. </p><h4>Choosing a compatible Interface Block</h4><p>If you select the <strong>Choose Compatible Interface Block</strong> solver, the <strong>Choose Proxy Port with Compatible Interface Block </strong>dialog opens.</p><p>You can now choose a Proxy Port and type it with an Interface Block that has Flow Properties compatible with the type and direction of the missing flow.</p><p>Alternatively, you can create a new Proxy Port and an Interface Block to type the Proxy Port. For this, select <strong>&lt;NEW&gt;</strong>.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="choosing_proxy_port_with_compatible_interface_block.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Handling inconsistencies between structure and behavior models" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Choosing a Proxy Port and specifying a compatible Interface Block as its type.</h6><h3>Handling missing Connectors</h3><p>The second validation rule checks whether there is a Connector between the two compatible Proxy Ports owned by the Part Property. The missing Connectors can be handled automatically using solvers.</p><p>To create missing Connectors</p><hr /><ol><li>Do one of the following:<ul><li>In the Internal Block Diagram, select a Proxy Port highlighted in yellow, and then in the smart manipulator toolbar, click <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="triangle_symbol_smart_manipulator_toolbar.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Handling inconsistencies between structure and behavior models" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="btos_second_validation_rule.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Handling inconsistencies between structure and behavior models" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>In the open <strong>Validation Results</strong> panel, right-click a validation result. <br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="second_validation_rule_creating_connector_from_validation_results_panel.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Handling inconsistencies between structure and behavior models" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ul></li><li>Select <strong>Create Connector</strong>.</li></ol><p>A Connector between the two compatible Proxy Ports is created. </p><h3>Handling missing Item Flows</h3><p>The third validation rule checks realized Item Flows in Activities and helps to ensure the correct Item Flows are realized on Connectors that connect Proxy Ports typed by compatible Interface Blocks. <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="72d79904-596f-45f5-a489-96b50cce33de">The</ac:inline-comment-marker> missing Item Flows can be handled automatically using solvers.</p><p>To realize missing Item Flows on Connectors</p><hr /><ol><li>Do one of the following:<ul><li>In the Internal Block Diagram, select a Connector highlighted in yellow, and then in the smart manipulator toolbar, click<ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="triangle_symbol_smart_manipulator_toolbar.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Handling inconsistencies between structure and behavior models" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="third_btos_validation_rule_IBD.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Handling inconsistencies between structure and behavior models" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>In the open <strong>Validation Results</strong> panel, right-click a validation result. <br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="third_btos_validation_rule_validation_results_panel.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Handling inconsistencies between structure and behavior models" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ul></li><li>Select one of the following:<ul><li><strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="57125055-8fc2-4cba-b681-24a478761656">Realize Missing Item Flow</ac:inline-comment-marker></strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="57125055-8fc2-4cba-b681-24a478761656"> - </ac:inline-comment-marker><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="57125055-8fc2-4cba-b681-24a478761656">realizes the compatible existing Item Flow on the Connector that was found in the behavior model.</ac:inline-comment-marker></li><li><strong>Open Item Flow Manager</strong> - opens the <strong><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Item Flow Manager dialog" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Item Flow Manager]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></strong> dialog.</li><li><strong>Change Item Flow Realization Compatibility Checking</strong> - opens the <strong>Project Options</strong> dialog to enable/disable the Item Flow realization checking. </li></ul></li></ol><p>A compatible Item Flow is realized on the selected Connector. </p><h3>Navigating to elements</h3><p>To learn more about the validated elements, in the smart manipulator toolbar of the Internal Block Diagram or Validation Results panel, select the <strong>Navigate To</strong> command to navigate to:</p><ul><li>Activity Edges in the Activity diagram from which the missing flow has been identified.</li><li>Part Properties in the Internal Block Diagram between which items are flowing.</li></ul><p>Elements are automatically selected on the diagram pane and in the Containment tree. </p><p> <ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="navigating_to_element_IBD.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Handling inconsistencies between structure and behavior models" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Navigating to an element from the smart manipulator toolbar.</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189146689 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00164: Hiding direction arrow on port shape

- page_id: `189146689`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189146689/Hiding+direction+arrow+on+port+shape
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-10-04T14:46:01.037+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > User Guide > Modeling structure with Blocks > Defining interfaces using ports > Creating ports
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To hide the direction arrow on [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Proxy Port' space=''] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Flow Port' space=''] shape

1. Right-click the Proxy Port or Flow Port shape and select the Symbol Properties .
2. In the Symbol Properties dialog, set the Show Port Direction property value to false.
3. Click Close . The direction arrow is hidden on the selected port shape.

The direction arrow of the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Full Port' space=''] shape is hidden by default. To display it, set the **Show Port Direction** property value to *true* in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Symbol Properties dialog' space='MD2024xR2']**Symbol Properties** dialog.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>To hide the direction arrow on <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Proxy Port" /></ac:link> or <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Flow Port" /></ac:link> shape</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click the Proxy Port or Flow Port shape and select the <strong>Symbol Properties</strong>.</li><li>In the <strong>Symbol Properties</strong> dialog, set the <strong>Show Port Direction</strong> property value to <em>false.</em></li><li>Click <strong>Close</strong>.<br />The direction arrow is hidden on the selected port shape.</li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a8be4db4-b70c-46f9-b22d-14251cd8a000"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The direction arrow of the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Full Port" /></ac:link> shape is hidden by default. To display it, set the <strong>Show Port Direction</strong> property value to <em>true</em> in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR2" ri:content-title="Symbol Properties dialog" /><ac:link-body><strong>Symbol Properties</strong> dialog</ac:link-body></ac:link>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189146690 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00165: Hiding the multiplicity

- page_id: `189146690`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189146690/Hiding+the+multiplicity
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-10-04T14:46:01.106+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > User Guide > Modeling structure with Blocks > Defining interfaces using ports > Creating ports
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can hide the multiplicity next to the port andPart Property type.

To hide the multiplicity

1. Right-click the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Flow Port' space=''], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Full Port' space=''], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Proxy Port' space=''], or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Part Property' space=''] shape and select**Symbol Properties**.
2. In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Symbol Properties dialog' space='MD2024xR2']**Symbol Properties** dialog, set the **Show Multiplicity** property value to *false*.
3. Click **Close**.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">You can hide the multiplicity next to the <span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">port and </span><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Part Property type</span>.</span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">To hide the multiplicity <br /></span></p><hr /><ol><li><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Right-click the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Flow Port" /></ac:link>, <span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"> <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Full Port" /></ac:link></span>, <span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Proxy Port" /></ac:link></span>, or <span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Part Property" /></ac:link></span> shape and select<strong> Symbol Properties</strong>.</span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">In the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR2" ri:content-title="Symbol Properties dialog" /><ac:link-body><strong>Symbol Properties</strong> dialog</ac:link-body></ac:link>, set the <strong>Show Multiplicity</strong> property value to <em>false</em>.</span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Click <strong>Close</strong>.</span></li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189147895 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00166: Humidifier example to Simulink model

- page_id: `189147895`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189147895/Humidifier+example+to+Simulink+model
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-10-04T14:46:20.856+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > Exporting to External Simulation Models > Simulink export > Simulating exported Simulink models
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This page describes how to simulate the *Humidifier*example model in Simulink. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Humidifier system sample' space='SYSMLP2024xR2']Learn more about *Humidifier*sample model >>

You can find the *Humidifier*sample model in:

- the modeling tool: Welcome window > Samples > Simulink and Modelica Transformation > Humidifier.
- the Installation directory: <modeling tool installation directory>\samples\SysML\Simulink and Modelica Transformation\*Humidifier**.*

To simulate the *Humidifier System* model

1. Export the HumidifierSystem Block to Simulink file. How to >>
2. In the Simulink Export Options dialog select the following options:
  - **Format**: XML (.slx)
  - **S-Function or Simscape**: S-Function version 2
  - Composite Signals : Bus Creators/Selectors
3. Make sure the MATLAB tool is installed.
4. Double-click the MATLAB icon to start it.
5. In the file directory select the location of your exported Humidifier Simulink files. They are displayed in the Current Folder panel. [ATTACHMENT filename='file_directory_simulink_files.png']
6. Double-click the file HumidifierSystem.slx . The model open in the Simulink window. [ATTACHMENT filename='simulink_window.png']
7. On the SIMULATION tab, click [ATTACHMENT filename='library_browser_button.png'] .
8. In the Simulink Library Browser dialog, select expand the Simulink group and select Sinks . See the figure below.
9. Drag and drop the Scope block onto the HumidifierSystem Simulink model. See the figure below.
10. Select signal line that connects the humidity-out port of the room block to the humidity-in port of the humidifier block and click on the Scope block. See the figure below. [ATTACHMENT filename='creating_scope_block.png']
11. On the SIMULATION tab, click the arrow on the PREPARE area and select the Model Settings . [ATTACHMENT filename='model_settings.png']
12. In the Configuration Parameters dialog, on the left side of the dialog select a Solver and specify the following on the right side of the dialog: - In the the Simulation time group change Start time to 0.0 , Stop time to 3600 . - In the Solver selection group select Type Variable-step or any other desirable solver that is suitable. - Click the arrow of the Solver details group and change the Max step size to 0.1 . [ATTACHMENT filename='configuration_parameters_dialog.png']
13. Press Apply > OK .
14. Double-click the Scope block in the model. An empty black plot opens.
15. Click button. In the Configuration Properties: Scope dialog, select the Logging tab and clear the Limit data points to last box. [ATTACHMENT filename='configuration_properties.png']
16. Press Apply > OK .
17. In the Scope plot toolbar, click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] and wait for the simulation results in the plot. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>This page describes how to simulate the <em>Humidifier </em>example model in Simulink. <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Humidifier system sample" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /><ac:link-body>Learn more about <em>Humidifier </em>sample model &gt;&gt;</ac:link-body></ac:link></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="92b70d06-6f5d-4d3c-aca6-61891a66027f"><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can f<span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">ind the <em>Humidifier </em>sample model in:</span></p><ul><li><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">the modeling tool: Welcome window &gt; Samples &gt; Simulink and Modelica Transformation &gt; Humidifier.</span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">the Installation directory: </span><em>&lt;modeling tool installation directory&gt;\samples\SysML\Simulink and Modelica Transformation\<em>Humidifier</em><em>.</em></em></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>To simulate the <em>Humidifier System</em> model</p><hr /><ol><li>Export the <em>HumidifierSystem </em>Block to Simulink file. <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/SYSMLP2024xR2/Simulink+export#Simulinkexport-ExportingmodeltotheSimulinkfile">How to &gt;&gt;</a></li><li>In the <strong>Simulink Export Options</strong><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> dialog</span> select the following options:<br /><ul class="diff-block-target"><li><p><strong>Format</strong>: XML (.slx)</p></li><li><p><strong>S-Function or Simscape</strong>: S-Function version 2</p></li><li><strong>Composite Signals</strong>: Bus Creators/Selectors</li></ul></li><li>Make sure the MATLAB tool is installed.</li><li>Double-click the MATLAB icon to start it.</li><li>In the file directory select the location of your exported Humidifier Simulink files. They are displayed in the <strong>Current Folder</strong> panel. <br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="file_directory_simulink_files.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Humidifier example to Simulink model" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>Double-click the file <strong>HumidifierSystem.slx</strong>. The model open in the <strong>Simulink</strong> window.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="simulink_window.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Humidifier example to Simulink model" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>On the <strong>SIMULATION</strong> tab, click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="71"><ri:attachment ri:filename="library_browser_button.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Humidifier example to Simulink model" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>. </li><li>In the <strong>Simulink Library Browser</strong> dialog, select expand the <strong>Simulink</strong> group and select <strong>Sinks</strong>. See the figure below.</li><li>Drag and drop the Scope block onto the <strong>HumidifierSystem </strong>Simulink model. See the figure below.</li><li>Select signal line that connects the<em> humidity-out</em> port of the room block to the<em> humidity-in</em> port of the<strong> </strong>humidifier block and click on the Scope block. See the figure below.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="creating_scope_block.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Humidifier example to Simulink model" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>On the <strong>SIMULATION</strong> tab, click the arrow on the <strong>PREPARE</strong> area and select the <strong>Model Settings</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="model_settings.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Humidifier example to Simulink model" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>In the <strong>Configuration Parameters</strong> dialog, on the left side of the dialog select a  <strong>Solver </strong>and specify the following on the right side of the dialog: <br /><strong>  </strong>-<strong> </strong>In the the <strong>Simulation time</strong> group change <strong>Start time </strong>to <strong>0.0</strong>, <strong>Stop time </strong>to <strong>3600</strong>.<br />  - In the <strong>Solver selection</strong> group select<strong> Type </strong><strong>Variable-step</strong> or any other desirable solver that is suitable.<br />  - Click the arrow of the <strong>Solver details</strong> group and change the <strong>Max step size</strong> to <strong>0.1</strong>.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="configuration_parameters_dialog.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Humidifier example to Simulink model" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>Press<strong> Apply</strong> &gt;<strong> OK</strong>.</li><li>Double-click the <strong>Scope</strong> block in the model. An empty black plot opens. </li><li>Click button. In the <strong>Configuration Properties: Scope </strong>dialog, select the <strong>Logging </strong>tab and clear the <strong>Limit data points to last </strong>box.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="configuration_properties.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Humidifier example to Simulink model" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>Press <strong>Apply </strong>&gt; <strong>OK</strong>.</li><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">In the Scope plot toolbar, click <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Run_button_matlab.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Humidifier example to Simulink model" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> and wait for the simulation results in the plot.</span><br /><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="simulation_results_plot.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Humidifier example to Simulink model" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /></span></li></ol><p><br /></p><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189147917 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00167: Humidifier example to Simulink model R2016a

- page_id: `189147917`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189147917/Humidifier+example+to+Simulink+model+R2016a
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2021-02-11T09:21:57.576+01:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > Exporting to External Simulation Models > Simulink export > Simulating exported Simulink models > Humidifier example to Simulink model
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To generate a Simulink model directly from the modeling tool

1. Right-click the Block TestCaseHumidifier::HumidifierSystem and select Tools > Export to Simulink. Please see Generating a simulation file file for more information.
2. Set the options listed below and click OK :
  - **Format**: XML (.sdl)
  - **S-Function or Simscape**: S-Function version 2
3. Launch Matlab with Simulink and Simscape extensions.
4. In Matlab’s Current Directory navigation bar, search for the file directory where the Simulink files (as well as the SysML files) are saved, and set it as the current directory. The Current Folder panel should display two generated Simulink “.slx” files. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
5. In the Current Folder panel, double-click on the file named Environment.slx . The model will open up in a Simulink window. Rearrange the blocks to reflect the signal flow arrowhead directions. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
6. Open the Library Browser either by using the 4-block icon or by going to Tools > Library Browser . Find and click on Sinks among the list in the Libraries panel, which will be categorized under Simulink . [IMAGE alt='' src='']
7. Pull-and-drag the Scope block from the Sinks library list into the Environment Simulink model. Attach a signal line segment from the Scope port onto the signal line that connects the humidity-out port of the room block to the humidity-in port of the humidifiersystem block. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
8. Go to Simulation > Model Configuration Settings . This allows selecting the types of solver and the runtime of the simulation. Select a Start Time of 0 , and a Stop Time of 3600 . Under Solver Options , select the solver Type to Variable-step (or any other desirable solver that is suitable) and change the Max step size to 0.01 . Press Apply , then press OK . [IMAGE alt='' src='']
9. Double-click the Scope block in the model. An empty (black) plot will show up. Press the ‘Scope’ parameters button (the gear icon above the empty plot). A ‘Scope’ parameters dialogue box will pop up. Go to the History tab and uncheck the option box Limit data points to last . Press Apply , and then OK . [IMAGE alt='' src='']
10. Go to Simulation > Run . Double-click on the Scope block to see the simulation results. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>To generate a Simulink model directly from the modeling tool </p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click the <em>Block</em><strong>TestCaseHumidifier::HumidifierSystem</strong> and select <strong>Tools </strong>&gt;<strong> Export</strong> to Simulink. Please see <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/SMTP190SP2/Generating+a+simulation+file" rel="nofollow">Generating a simulation file</a> file for more information.</li><li>Set the options listed below and click<strong> OK</strong>:<br /><ul><li><p><strong>Format</strong>: XML (.sdl)</p></li><li><p><strong>S-Function or Simscape</strong>: S-Function version 2</p></li></ul></li><li>Launch Matlab with Simulink and Simscape extensions.</li><li>In Matlab’s <strong>Current Directory</strong> navigation bar, search for the file directory where the Simulink files (as well as the SysML files) are saved, and set it as the current directory. The <strong>Current Folder</strong> panel should display two generated Simulink “.slx” files.<br /><br /><span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper confluence-embedded-manual-size"><ac:image ac:width="700"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Change_Directory_Matlab.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Humidifier example to Simulink model R2016a" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></span></li><li>In the <strong>Current Folder</strong> panel, double-click on the file named<strong> Environment.slx</strong>. The model will open up in a Simulink window. Rearrange the blocks to reflect the signal flow arrowhead directions.<br /><br /><span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper confluence-embedded-manual-size"><ac:image ac:width="600"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Environment_Simulink_Model.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Humidifier example to Simulink model R2016a" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></span></li><li>Open the<strong> Library Browser</strong> either by using the<strong> 4-block </strong>icon or by going to <strong>Tools</strong> &gt; <strong>Library Browser</strong>. Find and click on <strong>Sinks</strong> among the list in the <strong>Libraries</strong> panel, which will be categorized under<strong> Simulink</strong>.<br /><br /><span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper confluence-embedded-manual-size"><ac:image ac:width="550"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Searching_the_Simulink_Library_Browser.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Humidifier example to Simulink model R2016a" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></span></li><li>Pull-and-drag the <strong>Scope</strong> block from the <strong>Sinks</strong> library list into the <strong>Environment</strong> Simulink model. Attach a signal line segment from the <strong>Scope</strong> port onto the signal line that connects the<em> humidity-out</em> port of the room block to the<em> humidity-in</em> port of the<strong> humidifiersystem</strong> block.<br /><br /><span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper confluence-embedded-manual-size"><ac:image ac:width="700"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Attaching_a_Scope_Block.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Humidifier example to Simulink model R2016a" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></span></li><li>Go to <strong>Simulation</strong> &gt; <strong>Model Configuration Settings</strong>. This allows selecting the types of solver and the runtime of the simulation. Select a <strong>Start Time</strong> of <strong>0</strong>, and a <strong>Stop Time</strong> of <strong>3600</strong>. Under <strong>Solver Options</strong>, select the solver<strong> Type</strong> to <strong>Variable-step</strong> (or any other desirable solver that is suitable) and change the <strong>Max step size</strong> to <strong>0.01</strong>. Press<strong> Apply</strong>, then press<strong> OK</strong>.<br /><br /><span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper confluence-embedded-manual-size"><ac:image ac:width="750"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Simulink_Model_Configuration_Settings.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Humidifier example to Simulink model R2016a" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></span></li><li>Double-click the <strong>Scope</strong> block in the model. An empty (black) plot will show up. Press the <strong>‘Scope’ parameters</strong> button (the gear icon above the empty plot). A <strong>‘Scope’ parameters</strong> dialogue box will pop up. Go to the <strong>History</strong> tab and uncheck the option box <strong>Limit data points to last</strong>. Press <strong>Apply</strong>, and then <strong>OK</strong>.<br /><br /><span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper confluence-embedded-manual-size"><ac:image ac:width="700"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Configuring_the_Scope.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Humidifier example to Simulink model R2016a" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></span></li><li>Go to <strong>Simulation</strong> &gt; <strong>Run</strong>. Double-click on the <strong>Scope</strong> block to see the simulation results.<br /><br /><span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper confluence-embedded-manual-size"><ac:image ac:width="700"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Simulation_Results_for_Environment_Model.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Humidifier example to Simulink model R2016a" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></span></li></ol><p><span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper confluence-embedded-manual-size"><br /></span></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=220790847 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00168: Humidifier System example

- page_id: `220790847`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/220790847/Humidifier+System+example
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-04-01T11:09:27.489+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > Exporting to External Simulation Models > Modelica export > Simulating exported Modelica models
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This page describes how to simulate the *Humidifier*example model in Dymola. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Humidifier system sample' space='']Learn more about *Humidifier*sample model >>

You can find the *Humidifier*sample model in:

- the modeling tool: Welcome window > Samples > Simulink and Modelica Transformation > Humidifier.
- the Installation directory: <modeling tool installation directory>\samples\SysML\Simulink and Modelica Transformation\Humidifier.

To simulate the *Humidifier System* model

1. Export the HumidifierSystem Block to the Modelica file. To learn more, refer to the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Modelica export' space=''] page.
2. Download and install Dymole in your system. To learn more, refer to the Dymola page.
3. Launch the Dymola.
4. Click File > Open > Load... and select the saved *HumidifierSystem**.mo* file from your directory. The Humidifier System model is displayed in the **Package Browser**. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
5. In the Graphics tab toolbar, click Check to check for syntax and semantic errors in the model. The Logs panel displays whether the model is ready for simulation. [ATTACHMENT filename='Check Button.png']
6. Setup the simulation run for the model: 
 a. Select the******Simulation**tab and in its toolbar click [IMAGE alt='' src='']. 
b. In the **Simulation Setup** dialog, specify the **Start time******value to **0** and the **Stop time******value to**3600**seconds. 
c. Click **OK**. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
7. In the**Simulation** tab toolbar, click **Simulate**. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] When the simulation is completed, the output is displayed in the panel at the bottom and the *HumidifierSystem*variables are displayed in the **Variable Browser**.
8. To see the input and output of the EnvironmentModel , variables from the Variable Browser must be chosen. Selecting EnvironmentModel > _Environment > room > humidity_out will display the humidity level of the room overtime on the plotting window.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

The <strong>Logs </strong>panel displays whether the model is ready for simulation.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Check Button.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">Setup the simulation run for the model:<br />    a. Select the<strong> </strong><strong>Simulation </strong>tab and in its toolbar click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="21"><ri:attachment ri:filename="setup_button.png" /></ac:image>.</span><br /><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">    b. In the <strong>Simulation Setup</strong> dialog, specify the <strong>Start time</strong><strong> </strong>value to <strong>0</strong> and the <strong>Stop time</strong><strong> </strong>value to<strong> 3600 </strong>seconds.</span><br /><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">    c. Click <strong>OK</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Simulatio Setup.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></span></span></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">In the<strong> Simulation</strong> tab toolbar, click <strong>Simulate</strong>. <br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Simulate Button.png" /></ac:image></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c0ad6689-6aa0-43a3-b678-89d3d32e2be3"><ac:rich-text-body><p>When the simulation is completed, the output is displayed in the panel at the bottom and the <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> </span><em>HumidifierSystem </em>variables are displayed in the <strong>Variable Browser</strong>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">To see the input and output of the</span><strong> EnvironmentModel</strong><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">, variables from the </span><strong>Variable Browser</strong><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> must be chosen. Selecting </span><strong>EnvironmentModel</strong><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> &gt; </span><strong>_Environment</strong><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> &gt; </span><strong>room</strong><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> &gt; </span><strong>humidity_out</strong><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> will display the humidity level of the room overtime on the plotting window.</span></li></ol><p><br /></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189148007 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00170: Hydraulics example

- page_id: `189148007`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189148007/Hydraulics+example
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-10-04T14:46:22.915+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > Exporting to External Simulation Models > Modelica export > Simulating exported Modelica models
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This page describes how to simulate the *Hydraulics* example model in Dymola. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Hydraulics sample' space='SYSMLP2024xR2']Learn more about *Hydraulics*sample model >>

You can find the *Hydraulics*sample model in:

- the modeling tool: Welcome window > Samples > Simulink and Modelica Transformation > Hydraulics .
- the Installation directory: <modeling tool installation directory>\samples\SysML\Simulink and Modelica Transformation\*Hydraulics**.*

To simulate the *ConnectedTanks* model

1. Export the ConnectedTanks Block to Modelica file. How to >>
2. Make sure the Dymola tool is installed. How to >>
3. Start the Dymola.
4. Click File > Open > Load... and select saved ConnectedTanks .mo file from your file directory. A ConnectedTanks model is displayed in the Package Browser . [ATTACHMENT filename='connectedtanks_model_in_package_browser.png']
5. In the Graphics tab toolbar, click [ATTACHMENT filename='check_syntax_button.png'] to check model for syntax and semantic errors. The result panel displays whether the model is ready for simulation. [ATTACHMENT filename='check_syntax_connectedTanks.png']
6. Setup the simulation run for the model: a. Select the Simulation tab and in its toolbar click [ATTACHMENT filename='setup_button.png'] . b. In the Simulation Setup dialog, specify the Start time value to 0 and the Stop time value to 120 seconds (or any other reasonable amount of time; for this model, 120 seconds should be enough) . c. Click OK . [ATTACHMENT filename='simulation_setup_connectedTanks.png']
7. In the**Simulation** tab toolbar, click [IMAGE alt='' src='']. When the simulation is completed, the output is displayed in the panel at the bottom and the *ConnectedTanks***variables are displayed in the **Variable Browser**.
8. To see the input and output of the ConntectedTanks model , select variables in the**Variable Browser**as follows: - ConnectedTanks > _ConnectedTanks > fluidReservoir1 > fluidLevel . The changing fluid level of the first tank is displayed on the **Plot** window. See the following figure. - ConnectedTanks > _ConnectedTanks > fluidReservoir2 > fluidLevel . Thechanging fluid level of the second tank is displayed on the **Plot**window. See the following figure. [ATTACHMENT filename='plot_diagram_fluid_level.png']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>This page describes how to simulate the <em>Hydraulics</em> example model in Dymola. <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Hydraulics sample" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /><ac:link-body>Learn more about <em>Hydraulics </em>sample model &gt;&gt;</ac:link-body></ac:link></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4365f6e2-9efb-4b06-8ce8-7209854932d1"><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can f<span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">ind the <em>Hydraulics </em>sample model in:</span></p><ul><li><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">the modeling tool: Welcome window &gt; Samples &gt; Simulink and Modelica Transformation &gt; Hydraulics .</span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">the Installation directory: </span><em>&lt;modeling tool installation directory&gt;\samples\SysML\Simulink and Modelica Transformation\<em>Hydraulics</em><em>.</em></em></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>To simulate the <em>ConnectedTanks</em> model</p><hr /><ol><li>Export the <em>ConnectedTanks</em><em> </em>Block to Modelica file. <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/SYSMLP2024xR2/Modelica+export#Modelicaexport-ExportingmodeltotheModelicafile" rel="nofollow">How to &gt;&gt;</a></li><li>Make sure the Dymola tool is installed. <a class="external-link" href="https://www.3ds.com/products-services/catia/products/dymola/?woc=%7B%22category%22%3A%5B%22category%2Fdymola%22%5D%7D&amp;wockw=card_content_cta_1_url%3A%22https%3A%2F%2Fblogs.3ds.com%2Fcatia%2F%22" rel="nofollow">How to &gt;&gt;</a></li><li>Start the Dymola.</li><li>Click <strong>File</strong> &gt;<strong> Open </strong>&gt;<strong> Load... </strong>and select saved <em>ConnectedTanks</em><em>.mo</em> file from your file directory. A <em>ConnectedTanks</em><em> </em>model is displayed in the <strong>Package Browser</strong>.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="connectedtanks_model_in_package_browser.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Hydraulics example" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>In the <strong>Graphics </strong>tab toolbar, click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="24"><ri:attachment ri:filename="check_syntax_button.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="_buttons_dymola" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> to check model for syntax and semantic errors. The result panel displays whether the model is ready for simulation.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="check_syntax_connectedTanks.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Hydraulics example" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>Setup the simulation run for the model:<br />    a. Select the<strong> </strong><strong>Simulation </strong>tab and in its toolbar click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="21"><ri:attachment ri:filename="setup_button.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="_buttons_dymola" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>.<br />    b. In the <strong>Simulation Setup</strong> dialog, specify the <strong>Start time</strong><strong> </strong>value to <strong>0</strong> and the <strong>Stop time</strong><strong> </strong>value to<strong> 120 </strong>seconds <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">(or any other reasonable amount of time; for this model, 120 seconds should be enough)</span> .<br />    c. Click <strong>OK</strong>.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="simulation_setup_connectedTanks.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Hydraulics example" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">In the<strong> Simulation</strong> tab toolbar, click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="21"><ri:attachment ri:filename="simulate_button.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="_buttons_dymola" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>. </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1fd7759b-b761-4020-9824-4cc9a459d300"><ac:rich-text-body><p>When the simulation is completed, the output is displayed in the panel at the bottom and the <em>ConnectedTanks</em><em> </em>variables are displayed in the <strong>Variable Browser</strong>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">To see the input and output of the</span><strong> </strong><em>ConntectedTanks </em>model<span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">, <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">select variables in the </span><strong>Variable Browser </strong><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">as follows:</span></span><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> <br /></span><strong> </strong>-<strong> ConnectedTanks</strong><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> &gt; </span><strong>_ConnectedTanks</strong><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> &gt; </span><strong>fluidReservoir1</strong><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> &gt; </span><strong>fluidLevel</strong>. The<span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> changing fluid level of the <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">first tank is displayed</span> on the <strong>Plot</strong> window. See the following figure.<br /></span><strong> </strong>-<strong> ConnectedTanks</strong><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> &gt; </span><strong>_ConnectedTanks</strong><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> &gt; </span><strong>fluidReservoir2</strong><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> &gt;</span><strong> fluidLevel</strong><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">. The<span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> changing fluid level of the second tank is displayed on the <strong>Plot </strong>window. <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">See the following figure.</span></span></span><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="plot_diagram_fluid_level.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Hydraulics example" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><p /><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189147852 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00171: Hydraulics example to Simulink and Simscape model

- page_id: `189147852`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189147852/Hydraulics+example+to+Simulink+and+Simscape+model
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-10-04T14:46:20.634+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > Exporting to External Simulation Models > Simulink export > Simulating exported Simulink models
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This page describes how to simulate the *Hydraulics* example modelin Simulink. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Hydraulics sample' space='SYSMLP2024xR2']Learn more about *Hydraulics*sample model >>

You can find the *Hydraulics*sample model in:

- the modeling tool: Welcome window > Samples > Simulink and Modelica Transformation > Hydraulics .
- the Installation directory: <modeling tool installation directory>\samples\SysML\Simulink and Modelica Transformation\*Hydraulics**.*

To simulate the *Hydraulics* model

1. Export the ConnectedTanks Block to Simulink file. How to >>
2. In the Simulink Export Options dialog select the following options:
  - **Format**: XML (.slx)
  - **S-Function or Simscape**: Simscape
  - Simscape port libraries : Create new port types
  - Composite Signals : Bus Creators/Selectors
3. Make sure the MATLAB tool is installed.
4. Double-click the MATLAB icon to start it.
5. In the file directory select the location of your exported *Hydraulics* model Simulink files and choose the +ConnectedTanksLibrary folder. The simscape files (.ssc) are displayed in the Current Folder panel. [ATTACHMENT filename='file_directory_simulink_files_connectedTanks.png'] ssc_build
6. In the Command Window type ssc_build (or sscbuild depending on the Matlab version) and press Enter . This generates a folder sscprj in the +**ConnectedTanksLibrary** directory as well as a **ConnectedTanksLibrary**_lib.slx file in the directory with the original ConnectedTanks .slx file. [ATTACHMENT filename='command_window_connectedTanks.png']
7. Go back to the file directory, select the Hydraulics package with exported Simulink files and in the Current Folder select the ConnectedTanks.slx . [ATTACHMENT filename='current_folder_connectedTanks.png']
8. In the Current Folder panel, double-click on ConnectedTanks .slx to open the Simulink/Simscape model. Rearrange the blocks to get a better picture of the blocks and links. [ATTACHMENT filename='hydraulics_model_simscape.png']
9. On the **SIMULATION** tab, click the arrow on the **PREPARE** area and select the **Model Settings**. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
10. In the **Configuration Parameters** dialog, on the left side of the dialog: 
a. Select a **Solver**property group****and specify the following on the right side of the dialog: 
****-****In the the **Simulation time** group, change **Start time**to **0.0**, **Stop time**to **200**(or any other reasonable number of seconds). 
 - In the **Solver selection** group, select**Type****Variable-step** or any other desirable solver that is suitable. 
 - Click the arrow of the **Solver details** group and change the **Max step size** to **0.01**. b. Select Simscape property group and specify the following on the right side of the dialog: - In the Data Logging group, change Log simulation data to All . - Make sure Limit data points is unchecked. [ATTACHMENT filename='configuration_parameters_dialog_connectedTanks.png']
11. Press Apply > OK .
12. In the ConnectedTanks Simulink window, click [IMAGE alt='' src=''].
13. On the main toolbar, click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] to open a Simscape Results Explorer : ConnectedTanks window where you can expand the ConnectedTanks blocks to see how their properties simulated through time. For the tanks fluidReservoir1 and fluidReservoir2 , the properties fluidLevel can be selected to see how the fluid reacted transferred from one tank to the other in the simulation. To see the simulation’s specific data points, select the mouse pointer on the plot. [ATTACHMENT filename='simscape_results_explorer_connectedTanks.png']
14. To change the default value or initial value parameters of the model, double-click the blocks in the ConnectedTanks Simulink model. A Block Parameters dialog opens where you can change parameters. Repeat steps from 5 to 12 to run the simulation with (new) block parameters. [ATTACHMENT filename='fluiReservoir2_block_prameters.png']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189147878 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00172: Hydraulics example to Simulink and Simscape model R2016a

- page_id: `189147878`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189147878/Hydraulics+example+to+Simulink+and+Simscape+model+R2016a
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2021-02-11T09:21:08.056+01:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > Exporting to External Simulation Models > Simulink export > Simulating exported Simulink models > Hydraulics example to Simulink and Simscape model
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To generate a Simulink model directly from the modeling tool

1. Right-click the *Block* **Data:ConnectedTanks** and select **Tools**> **Export to Simulink.** Please see [Generating a simulation file](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/SMTP190SP2/Generating+a+simulation+file) for more information.
2. Set the options listed below and click **OK**:
  - **Format**: XML (.sdl)
  - **S-Function or Simscape**: Simscape
  - **Simscape port libraries**: Create new port types
3. Launch Matlab with Simulink and Simscape extensions.
4. In Matlab’s Current Directory navigation bar, search for the file directory where the Simulink/Simscape files (as well as the SysML files) are saved, and set it as the current directory. The Current Folder panel should display the generated files.
5. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
6. In the **Current Folder** panel, double-click on the folder named**+ConnectedTanksLibrary_create** (this sets the current directory to this folder). Type **ssc_build** in the**Command Window** and press Enter. This will generate a folder **sscprj** in the **+ConnectedTanksLibrary_create** directory as well as a **ConnectedTanksLibrary_create_lib.slx** file in the directory with the original **ConnectedTanks.slx** file. Next, once again, search for and go back to the file directory where the Simulink/Simscape files (as well as the SysML files) are saved, and set it as the current directory. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
7. In the **Current Folder** panel, double-click on **ConnectedTanks.slx** to open the Simulink/Simscape model. Rearrange the blocks to get a better picture of the blocks and links. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
8. Go to **Simulation** > **Model Configuration Settings**. This allows to select the types of solver and the runtime of the simulation. Select a **Start Time** of **0**, and a **Stop Time** of **200** (or any other reasonable number of seconds). Change the **Max step size** to **0.01**. Under **Solver Options**, keep the solver **Type** to **Variable-step** (or any other desirable solver that is suitable). Press **Apply**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
9. In Model Configuration Settings, select **Simscape** in the**Commonly Used Parameters** panel. Change **Log simulation data** to **All**. Make sure**Limit data points**is unchecked. Press **Apply**. Press **OK**. [ATTACHMENT filename='Model_Configuration_Settings.png']
10. In the ConnectedTanks Simulink window, go to Simulation > Run .
11. Once the model has compiled, go to the Matlab’s command window and type/enter ssc_explore(simlog) (make sure that either the version of Matlab has the function ssc_explore , or a file ssc_explore.m is in the same directory as ConnectedTanks.slx in the Current Folder panel). A Simscape data logging explorer: Connected Tanks window will open up. [ATTACHMENT filename='Ssc_explore(simlog)_command_and_the_Simscape_data_logging_explorer.png']
12. In the Simscape data logging explorer , the ConnectedTanks blocks can be expanded to see how their properties simulated through time. For the tanks fluidReservoir1 and fluidReservoir2 , the properties fluidLevel can be selected to see how the fluid reacted transferred from one tank to the other in the simulation. To see the simulation’s specific data points, the Data cursor icon needs to be selected and points on the plot need to be chosen (by clicking it). [ATTACHMENT filename='Using_the_Simscape_data_loggng_explorer.png']
13. To change the default value or initial value parameters of the model, double-click the blocks in the ConnectedTanks Simulink model. A window will pop out for block parameters that can be changed. Run through Steps 7 through 10 to run the simulation with (new) block parameters again. [ATTACHMENT filename='Changing_block_parameters.png']

Press <strong>Apply</strong>.<br /></span><br /><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><ac:image ac:width="800"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Simulink_Simscape_Model_Configuration_Settings.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Hydraulics example to Simulink and Simscape model R2016a" ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /></span><br /><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><br /></span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">In Model Configuration Settings, select <strong>Simscape</strong> in the<strong> Commonly Used Parameters</strong> panel. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189146711 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00175: Implied Connectors

- page_id: `189146711`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189146711/Implied+Connectors
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-10-04T14:46:01.932+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > User Guide > Modeling structure with Blocks > Connecting Blocks in SysML Internal Block Diagram
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1013125501

1013125503

1013125502

The implied relation functionality allows you to analyze existing relations among parts in the Internal Block Diagrams. It let you know that parts are somehow related with each other without giving the detailed view of actual Connectors and ports. You can display implied relations between parts which are connected through ports/interfaces. By default, the implied relation is represented as a dashed line with a short summary in label of how many actual connectors are underneath. You can specify a different style of the implied relation by using its the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Symbol Properties dialog' space='MD2024xR2']**Symbol Properties** dialog.

The implied relation functionality allows you to:

- Display implied relations between [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Part Property' space=''] through ports/interfaces.
- Display actual [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Connector' space='MD2024xR2'] and ports from the implied relations level by level or all at once.
- Find actual Connectors in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Containment tab' space='MD2024xR2'] directly from the implied relation.
- Specify [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Symbol Properties dialog' space='MD2024xR2'] of the implied relation.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### On the left side: the implied relation shows that 18 connectors exist between TV and TV Tuner parts. On the right side: all 18 actual connectors and ports are shown by using the Semantic Zoom In, or Display Underlying Connectors command.

How to work with implied Connectors read in the following pages:

1013125499

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Internal Block Diagram' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Connecting parts through interface' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Hiding direction arrow on port shape' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying parts and ports' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Autowire Parts' space='']

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Enforce Ports Compatibility mode' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="aa88555c-5123-42b8-8ac1-dac302263cf3"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1013125501</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b74925e0-9630-46cf-b161-77c76d38ad04"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1013125503</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="15b2b186-e9c8-4a36-8f80-a3495b30126c"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1013125502</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The implied relation functionality allows you to analyze existing relations among parts in the Internal Block Diagrams. It let you know that parts are somehow related with each other without giving the detailed view of actual Connectors and ports. You can display implied relations between parts which are connected through ports/interfaces. By default, the implied relation is represented as a dashed line with a short summary in label of how many actual connectors are underneath. You can specify a different style of the implied relation by using its the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR2" ri:content-title="Symbol Properties dialog" /><ac:link-body><strong>Symbol Properties</strong> dialog</ac:link-body></ac:link>.</p><p>The implied relation functionality allows you to:</p><ul><li>Display implied relations between <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Part Property" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Part Properties]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> <s> </s>through ports/interfaces.</li><li>Display actual <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR2" ri:content-title="Connector" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Connectors]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> and ports from the implied relations level by level or all at once.</li><li>Find actual Connectors in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR2" ri:content-title="Containment tab" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Containment tree]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> directly from the implied relation.</li><li>Specify <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR2" ri:content-title="Symbol Properties dialog" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[symbol properties]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> of the implied relation.</li></ul><p><span style="color: rgb(255,0,0);"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="implied_relations_zoom_in.png" /></ac:image></span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">On the left side: the implied relation shows that 18 connectors exist between TV and TV Tuner parts. On the right side: all 18 actual connectors and ports are shown by using the Semantic Zoom In, or Display Underlying Connectors command.</h6><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">How to work with implied Connectors read in the following pages:</span></span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="7e1419f1-df1f-448f-92b2-d1448b471e51" /><br /></span></span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><br /></span></span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="34acb636-fdd6-40c4-97af-036b9771ee0b"><ac:parameter <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189146793 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00181: Initial Values

- page_id: `189146793`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189146793/Initial+Values
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-10-04T14:46:03.342+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > User Guide > Modeling structure with Blocks
- labels: ['initial-values', 'context-specific-values', 'default-values']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1345490194

1345490196

1345490195

A [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Compartments' space='MD2024xR2'] with a label of *initial values* is used to show values of properties belonging to a containing block. These values override any default values that may have been previously specified on these properties on their originally defining block. Initial value compartments may be specified within nested properties, which then apply only in the particular usage context defined by the outermost containing block.

The purpose of initial value compartments is to show various values as a result of a reconfigured selected context. Initial value compartments allows to:

- Create different configurations for the same structure and display them directly in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Internal Block Diagram' space=''] .
- Have different values for the same part in different contexts.
- Assign a different initial value to an inherited property.

The *initial value*s compartment can be shown on [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Part Property' space=''] shape compartment and display the values of the properties reconfigured in a selected context.

The procedures below describes how to work with initial values and its compartments:

43

##### Defining initial values

You can define context-specific initial values by overriding any default values that was previously specified on Value Properties on their originally defining Block. Before defining a context-specific initial value, the followingprerequisites must be met:

- The Value Property must be created for a Block which is a type of Part Property.
- The Type and Default Value are defined for the Value Property in its Specification window.

To defineinitial value

1. Right-click the Part Property shape with default value defined.
2. From the shortcut menu, select Tools > **Define Context-Specific Initial Value**. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. From the **Select Property** dialog, select Value Property which you want to redefine. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] After selecting the Value Property, the structure of instances is created automatically in the Model Browser.
4. Type the value directly on the Part Property shape. If you can not specify the new value, check if the context of that Part Property is correct. ]]>****
5. Press Enter. The slot with newly defined value is created for the Instance Specification. The default value is not changed. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Displaying/hiding *initial values* compartments

You can display/hide initial values on the Part Property Compartments. Read more about [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML specific compartments' space=''].

To show/hide Initial Values on Part Property compartment, do one of the following

- Select the Part Property shape, click the Compartments [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Smart manipulators' space='MD2024xR2'] , and select Initial Values. [ATTACHMENT filename='initial_value_compartments.png']
- Right-click the Part Property shape and from the shortcut menu select Symbol Properties. In the Symbol Properties dialog, select the Expert or All mode, and set the Suppress Context Specific Values property value to true to show initial values, or false - to hide them.

##### contextSelecting the context of initial values

Initial values which are shown in the Part Property compartments depends from the selected context. For example, if the selected context is A then the compartment show values of A.

To select a context of initial values

1. Right-click the Part Property shape and from the shortcut menu select Symbol Properties.
2. In the Symbol Properties dialog, select the All mode.
3. Select the Context of Context-Specific Values property value box, and click [ATTACHMENT filename='edit_value_button_in_specification_window.png'] .
4. From the Select Element dialog, choose the element which will be the context.
5. Click OK .
6. Click Close . The context for initial values is specified.

##### Displaying/hiding element type in the *initial values* compartments

You can display or hide the elements types in the *initial values* compartments of a part using either the Symbol(s) Properties dialog or the part shortcut menu.

To display/hide element type in the *initial values* compartments

1. Right-click the Part Property shape and select the Symbol Properties command.
2. In the Symbol Properties dialog, select the Expert or All mode.
3. Select the Show Slot Type property value from drop-down list:

- 
  - None - to hide types.
  - Name - to display names of the element types.
  - Qualified Name - to display qualified names of the element types.

1345490192

**Related pages**

**Sample model**

The model used in the figures of this page is the **Phone Camera** sample model. To open this sample download [ATTACHMENT filename='PhoneCamerasInitialValues.mdzip'].

style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">structure of instances is created automatically in the Model Browser</span>.</span></span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Type the value directly on the Part Property shape.<br /></span></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3b4c080f-31ca-4155-96c2-47d992684b8a"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If you can not specify the new value<span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">, check if the context of that Part Property is correct. <ac:link ac:anchor="context"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[How to select the context of initial value >]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></span><strong> </strong></span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Press Enter.</span></span><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><br /></span></span>The slot with newly defined value is created for the Instance Specification. The default value is not changed.<br /><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="defined_initial_value.png" /></ac:image><br /></span></span></li></ol><h3>Displaying/hiding <em>initial values</em> compartments</h3><p>You can display/hide initial values on the Part Property Compartments. Read more about <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML specific compartments" /></ac:link>.</p><p>To show/hide Initial Values on Part Property compartment, do one of the following</p><hr /><ul><li>Select the Part Property shape, click the Compartments <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR2" ri:content-title="Smart manipulators" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[smart manipulator]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, and select Initial Values.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="initial_value_compartments.png" /></ac:image></li><li>Right-click the Part Property shape and from the shortcut menu select Symbol Properties. In the <strong>Symbol Properties</strong> dialog, select the <strong>Expert</strong> or <strong>All</strong> mode, and set the <strong>Suppress Context Specific Values</strong> property value to true to show initial values, or false - to hide them.</li></ul><p><br /></p><h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e05be940-64f5-4589-a6ba-a9ee129722b4"><ac:parameter ac:name="">context</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>Selecting the context of initial values</h3><p>Initial values which are shown in the Part Property compartments depends from the selected context. For example, if the selected context is A then the compartment show values of A.</p><p>To select a context of initial values</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click the Part Property shape and from the shortcut menu select Symbol Properties.</li><li>In the <strong>Symbol Properties</strong> dialog, select the <strong>All</strong> mode.</li><li>Select the <strong>Context of Context-Specific Values</strong> property value box, and click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="18"><ri:attachment ri:filename="edit_value_button_in_specification_window.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="_buttons" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>.</li><li>From the <strong>Select Element</strong> dialog, choose the element which will be the context.</li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong>.</li><li>Click <strong>Close</strong>.<br />The context for initial values is specified.</li></ol><h3>Displaying/hiding element type in the <em>initial values</em> compartments</h3><p>You can display or hide the elements types in the <em>initial values</em> compartments of a part using either the Symbol(s) Properties dialog or the part shortcut menu.</p><p>To display/hide element type in the <em>initial values</em> compartments</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click the Part Property shape and select the <strong>Symbol Properties</strong> command.</li><li>In the <strong>Symbol Properties</strong> dialog, select the <strong>Expert</strong> or <strong>All</strong> mode.</li><li>Select the <strong>Show Slot Type</strong> property value from drop-down list:</li></ol><ul><li style="list-style-type: none;background-image: none;"><ul><li><strong>None</strong> - to hide types.</li><li><strong>Name</strong> - to display names of the element types.</li><li><strong>Qualified Name</strong> - to display qualified names of the element types.</li></ul></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="70fa2bb3-0cc2-4788-8fe3-6c09aa836f15"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1345490192</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_equal"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="08d7c3dc-6584-461a-8cf3-c0be512dea3e" /></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Sample model</strong></p><p class="auto-cursor-target">The model used in the figures of this page is the <strong>Phone Camera</strong> sample model. To open this sample download <ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="PhoneCamerasInitialValues.mdzip"><ri:page ri:content-title="_sample models" /></ri:attachment></ac:link>.</p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189147976 space=SYSMLP2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00183: Instance Specifications export to the Modelica file

- page_id: `189147976`
- space_key: `SYSMLP2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSMLP2024xR2/pages/189147976/Instance+Specifications+export+to+the+Modelica+file
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-10-04T14:46:21.578+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML Plugin Documentation > Exporting to External Simulation Models > Modelica export
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

185593144

185593146

185593145

185593143

You can export to the Modelica file a particular configuration with initial values and subtype instances as specified in the instance model. The mechanism generates Modelica (.mo) file with an Instance Specification name and save it to your file directory. In the following figure, the *p* Instance Specification is exported to the Modelica file.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The export of*p* Instance Specification to the Modelica file.

****

To export a configuration with initial values

1. In the Model Browser , select an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Instance Specification' space='MD2024xR2'] you want to export and do one of the following:
  - In the top-left corner of the modeling tool, click File > Export To > Modelica .
  - Right-click the selected Instance Specification and then click Tools > Export to Modelica .
2. Specify export options in the Modelica Export Options dialog. Option descriptions >>
3. Click OK . The Modelica (.mo) file is generated and saved to your file directory.

185597894

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE
